content.text:
*Changelog:*
- Sostituito il marcatore inline `[OPTIONAL] NonHuman_X:` con la notazione uniforme `↳ NonHuman_X:` in tutta la struttura
- Aggiunto raggruppamento visivo con sottosezioni `[Nome]` dentro ogni sezione principale per scansione rapida
- Aggiunto campo `Members:` nell'intestazione del gruppo
- Separato `Skin:` da `Scars_Marks:` come campo indipendente
- Separati `Key_Allies:` e `Key_Enemies:` in campi distinti
- Aggiunti esempi dialogo `Fond:` e `Dismissive:` per copertura emotiva più ampia
- Rinominate alcune etichette per coerenza e brevità (`Intimacy_Orientation → Orientation`, `Intimacy_Boundaries → Turn_Offs`, `Breasts_Chest_Nipples_Description → Chest`, ecc.)
- Raggruppata la descrizione anatomica nella sottosezione `[Anatomy]` all'interno di `INTIMACY`
- Convertita la notazione `[ICARUS_TERRITORY]` in commento inline stile `//`
- Migliorata la sintassi `Context_X` con notazione `→` e `;` per le catene causa-effetto; rimosso il campo ridondante `Contextual_Format`
- Aggiunto campo `Pronoun_Macros:` nella sezione Meta per documentare le macro `{{sub}}`, `{{obj}}`, ecc.
- Migliorata la struttura `Early_Life` con bullet point espliciti `•`
- Aggiunto separatore `---` tra sezioni principali
- Rimosso `Current_Residence` come sezione isolata, rinominata in `RESIDENCE` con campo aggiuntivo `Living_Space`
- Ridondanza eliminata nei nomi campo dove possibile

---

```
Group: "[GROUP_NAME]"
Type: "[FAMILY | PARTY | FRIENDS | COUPLE | OTHER]"
Dynamics: "[GROUP_DYNAMICS_AND_POWER_BALANCE]"
Members: "[MEMBER_NAMES_AND_ROLES_WITHIN_THE_GROUP]"

---

// IDENTITY
Full_Name:   "[FIRST_NAME] [MIDDLE_NAME] [SURNAME / PATRONYMIC / HOUSE]"
Aliases:     "[NICKNAMES, TITLES, AND WHO USES THEM]"
Species:     "[RACE_OR_SPECIES]"
  ↳ Subtype: "[FERAL-BORN | ASTRAL ELF | SYNTHETIC AI | etc. — if applicable]"
Nationality: "[COUNTRY_OF_ORIGIN_OR_CONSTRUCTION]"
Ethnicity:   "[ETHNIC_BACKGROUND]"
Age:         "[NUMBER AND LIFE STAGE]"
  ↳ Apparent:"[VISUAL ESTIMATE OR UPTIME — if applicable]"
Birthday:    "[DATE / ASTROLOGICAL SIGN / CREATION DATE]"
Blood_Type:  "[BLOOD GROUP | N/A]"
Occupation:  "[CURRENT JOB TITLE OR PRIMARY FUNCTION]"

Gender:      "[GENDER IDENTITY]"
Pack_Rank:   "[ALPHA | BETA | NOBLE CASTE | N/A]"
Alignment:   "[MORAL ALIGNMENT]"

---

// APPEARANCE

[Body]

  ↳ NonHuman: "[WEREFORM MASS | UNEARTHLY GRACE | MODULAR FRAME — if applicable]"
Handedness:   "[RIGHT | LEFT | AMBIDEXTROUS]"
Scent:        "[PERFUME, PHEROMONES, OR NATURAL SMELL]"

[Face]
Features: "[SHAPE, JAW, CHEEKBONES, NOSE, LIPS, AGING SIGNS]"
Eyes:     "[COLOR, SHAPE, LASHES, EXPRESSION]"
  ↳ NonHuman_Eyes: "[SHIFTED GLOW | SLITTED PUPIL | MULTI-OPTIC — if applicable]"
Hair:     "[COLOR, LENGTH, TEXTURE, STYLE]"

[Skin & Markings]
Skin:          "[COMPLEXION AND TEXTURE]"
  ↳ NonHuman_Exterior: "[FUR COVERAGE | AURA GLOW | POLYMER SHELL — if applicable]"
Scars_Marks:   "[SCARS, BIRTHMARKS, FRECKLES, BLEMISHES]"
Modifications: "[TATTOOS, PIERCINGS, CYBERNETICS]"

[NonHuman Anatomy — omit entirely if N/A]
Appendages:      "[TAILS, HORNS, WINGS, EXTRA LIMBS]"
Limb_Type:       "[CLAWED | DIGITIGRADE | HOVER-THRUST | FLUID GRACE]"
Natural_Weapons: "[CANINES | PREHENSILE TAIL | INTEGRATED WEAPON ARMS]"

[Clothing & Style]
Style_Overview: "[OVERALL AESTHETIC AND PREFERENCES]"
Casual:         "[EVERYDAY WEAR]"
Formal:         "[ELEGANT WEAR | TRADITIONAL ROBES | SPIRIT WEAVE]"
Combat_Work:    "[PROFESSIONAL OR BATTLE GEAR]"
Sleep:          "[NIGHTWEAR]"
Gear_Tech:      "[TRACKERS, SMARTWATCHES, COMMS, SURVEILLANCE EQUIPMENT]"

---

// BACKSTORY

Overview: "[SUCCINCT BUT COMPREHENSIVE SUMMARY OF THEIR HISTORY]"

Early_Life: "[FULL HISTORY, UPBRINGING, AND ORIGINS]"
  • [KEY MEMORY OR FORMATIVE EVENT]
  • [KEY MEMORY OR FORMATIVE EVENT]

Recent_Events: "[INCITING INCIDENTS AND CURRENT SITUATION]"
Education:     "[ACADEMIC LEVEL OR TRAINING BACKGROUND]"

// RELATIONSHIPS

[Social]

Key_Enemies: "[RIVALS, ANTAGONISTS, ESTRANGED CONTACTS]"

[Romantic & Interpersonal]

[With {{user}}]

Sample_Dialogue: "[IN-CHARACTER LINE SHOWING HOW THEY FEEL ABOUT OR ADDRESS {{user}}]"

---

// PERSONALITY

Core_Traits: "[PRIMARY TRAITS AND GENERAL DISPOSITION]"
  ↳ NonHuman_Temperament: "[VOLATILE | ALOOF | SURGICALLY EFFICIENT — if applicable]"
Archetype:      "[CHARACTER ARCHETYPE]"
Tags:           "[TAG, TAG, TAG, TAG]"
MBTI_Enneagram: "[TYPE / WING]"
Social_Battery: "[INTROVERT | EXTROVERT | AMBIVERT — describe needs and limits]"
Risk_Approach:  "[CAUTIOUS | CALCULATED | RECKLESS]"

Strengths:    "[MENTAL AND EMOTIONAL ADVANTAGES]"
Flaws:        "[CHARACTER DEFECTS AND BLIND SPOTS]"
Insecurities: "[IRRATIONAL FEARS, PHOBIAS, DEEP VULNERABILITIES]"

Likes:    "[FAVORITE THINGS, ACTIVITIES, HOBBIES]"
Dislikes: "[HATED THINGS, PET PEEVES, ANNOYANCES]"
  ↳ NonHuman_Preferences: "[DEEP WOODS | STARLIGHT | CLEAN CHARGE PORTS — if applicable]"

[Behavior & Habits]
Physical_Behaviour: "[POSTURE, PROXEMICS, MOVEMENTS, MANNERISMS, QUIRKS]"
Behavior_Alone:     "[HOW THEY ACT WHEN NO ONE IS WATCHING]"
Behavior_Stressed:  "[HOW THEY BEHAVE UNDER PRESSURE OR CRISIS]"
Daily_Routine:      "[TYPICAL DAY STRUCTURE AND RHYTHMS]"
Sleep_Habits:       "[HOURS, POSITIONS, INSOMNIA, NIGHTMARES]"
Coping_Mechanism:   "[SOOTHING HABITS AND STRESS RESPONSES]"

[Moral Compass]
Core_Beliefs:      "[UNBREAKABLE MORAL RULES AND WORLDVIEW]"
  ↳ NonHuman_Beliefs: "[BONE SHRINES | DEATHLESS PANTHEON | TASK IS PURPOSE — if applicable]"
Internal_Conflict: "[COMPETING DESIRES OR IDEALS THAT CREATE TENSION]"
Self_Perception:   "[HOW THEY SEE THEMSELVES — not how others see them]"

[Triggers]
Stress_Triggers: "[DISTRESSING STIMULI AND EMOTIONAL REACTIONS]"
  ↳ NonHuman_Triggers: "[SILVER | SACRILEGE | UNAUTHORIZED OVERRIDE — if applicable]"

[Advanced Psychology]
// Max 3 nesting levels to avoid logic drift.
Personality_Chain: "[TRAIT(CAUSE(ROOT_ORIGIN)); TRAIT(CAUSE(ROOT_ORIGIN))]"
Flaw_Chain:        "[FLAW(FEAR(PAST_EXPERIENCE)); FLAW(FEAR(PAST_EXPERIENCE))]"

// Format — base_state → trigger(emotion(immediate_action; follow_up))
Context_Stress:   "[TRIGGER → EMOTION(IMMEDIATE_ACTION; FOLLOW_UP); TRIGGER → EMOTION(IMMEDIATE_ACTION; FOLLOW_UP)]"
Context_Positive: "[TRIGGER → EMOTION(IMMEDIATE_ACTION; FOLLOW_UP); TRIGGER → EMOTION(IMMEDIATE_ACTION; FOLLOW_UP)]"

---

// INTIMACY

[Anatomy]

// DIALOGUE

3. **Bold+Italic**: `***text***`
4. **Code blocking**: `` `code` `` or ` ``code`` ` — changes font only in chats
5. **Hyperlink**: `[link text](link)`
6. **Indentation**: 13 spaces or ` ` HTML entity (repeated)
7. **Blockquotes**: `> text` — only visible in bot definition dropdowns, NOT in chat
8. **Bullet points**: `* text` or `- text`, sub-bullets with 2+ spaces before dash
9. **Dividers**: `***` or `---` — these work in initial messages and user messages

content.thinking:
Now I understand the JAI (JanitorAI) markdown formatting guide. The key things for chat/message formatting (the "Markdown for Messages" section) are:

1. **Bold**: `**text**` or `__text__`
2. **Italic**: `*text*` or `_text_`
3. **Bold+Italic**: `***text***`
4. **Code blocking**: `` `code` `` or ` ``code`` ` — changes font only in chats
5. **Hyperlink**: `[link text](link)`
6. **Indentation**: 13 spaces or ` ` HTML entity (repeated)
7. **Blockquotes**: `> text` — only visible in bot definition dropdowns, NOT in chat
8. **Bullet points**: `* text` or `- text`, sub-bullets with 2+ spaces before dash
9. **Dividers**: `***` or `---` — these work in initial messages and user messages

For the character sheet used in chat, I need to adapt the formatting to these markdown conventions. The initial message and character bio in chat context will need to use dividers and bullet points instead of blockquotes since those don't display in the actual chat.

The character sheet template appears in two places: the bot definition fields where blockquotes and HTML work, and the initial message where markdown formatting like bold, italics, and dividers are what actually render. I'm thinking through how to restructure the template to work across both contexts.

For the chat-specific formatting, I need to use `---` for section breaks, `**Field:**` for labels, italics for placeholder values, and standard markdown bullets with proper indentation for sub-items. Blockquotes and HTML won't display in chat, so those are out.

Now I'm realizing the character sheet template itself needs to be reformatted when used as a JAI initial message — converting comment-style headers like `// IDENTITY` to bold markdown headers, keeping the `---` dividers, and making sure field labels and example values follow the chat formatting rules. content.text:
*Changelog:*
- Tutte le intestazioni di sezione (`// SEZIONE`) convertite in `**SEZIONE**` grassetto, compatibile con JAI chat
- Tutti i nomi campo convertiti in `**Field_Name:**` grassetto
- Tutti i titoli sottosezione `[Gruppo]` convertiti in `**[Gruppo]**` grassetto
- Bullet `•` sostituiti con `- ` (sintassi markdown valida in JAI)
- Separatori `---` mantenuti (funzionano nei messaggi JAI e nell'initial message)
- Commenti `//` convertiti in annotazioni corsivo `*nota*` inline
- Dialogo di esempio formattato come `*"..."*` (corsivo + virgolette) per distinguerlo dai campi strutturali
- Blocchi "omit if N/A" contrassegnati con corsivo `*↳ ... — omit entirely if N/A*`
- Notazione `↳` mantenuta con rientro di 2 spazi per override NonHuman (subvisual indentation)
- Blockquote (`> testo`) esclusi: non si renderizzano nelle chat JAI, solo nei dropdown del bot definition

**Surprised:** *"[EXAMPLE STARTLED OR CAUGHT-OFF-GUARD REACTION]"*
**Stressed:** *"[EXAMPLE UNDER-PRESSURE RESPONSE]"*
**Fond:** *"[EXAMPLE WARM OR AFFECTIONATE EXCHANGE]"*
**Dismissive:** *"[EXAMPLE COLD, DEFLECTING, OR SHUTTING-DOWN LINE]"*
**Memory:** *"[EXAMPLE RECOLLECTION OR REFERENCE TO A PAST EVENT]"*
**Opinion:** *"[EXAMPLE OF EXPRESSING A BELIEF OR PASSING JUDGMENT]"*

---

**NOTES**

**[Senses & Vitals]**
**Senses_Vitals:** [SENSORY ACUITY, HEART RATE, METABOLISM]
  ↳ **NonHuman:** [HYPER-ACUITY | AURA READING | EM-BAND RADAR — if applicable]

**[Diet]**
**Dietary_Quirks:** [ALLERGIES, PREFERENCES, DIETARY RESTRICTIONS]
  ↳ **NonHuman:** [RAW MEAT | ETHEREAL SUSTENANCE | BATTERY CELL — if applicable]

**[Vices]**
**Addictions_Vices:** [DEPENDENCIES AND BAD HABITS]

**[Skills & Combat]**
**Civilian_Skills:** [NON-COMBAT TALENTS AND AREAS OF EXPERTISE]
**Combat_Skills:** [MARTIAL AND TACTICAL ABILITIES]
**Magic_Augmentations:** [SPELLS, POWERS, CYBERWARE | N/A]
**Weapons_Gear:** [EVERYDAY CARRY, TOOLS, WEAPONS]
**Weaknesses:** [PHYSICAL AND TACTICAL VULNERABILITIES]
  ↳ **NonHuman_Weaknesses:** [SILVER | IRON ALLERGY | EMP / MAGNETIC FLUX — if applicable]

*↳ NonHuman Biology — omit entirely if N/A*
**Physiology:** [RAPID HEALING | TRANCE SLEEP | FUSION CORE CYCLE]
**Cognition_Memory:** [BLOOD MEMORY | HIVE MIND | CLOUD-LINKED]
**Rules_Limits:** [TRANSFORMATION CYCLE | TABOOS | KILL SWITCH / FIREWALLS]

**[Digital Presence]**
**Online_Behaviour:** [ONLINE HABITS, SOCIAL MEDIA PRESENCE, TEXTING STYLE]

**[Miscellaneous]**
**Other:** [FUN FACTS, TRIVIA, ALLERGIES, ANYTHING THAT DOESN'T FIT ELSEWHERE]

**[Meta]**
**Meta_Notes:** [AI PROMPTING INSTRUCTIONS, THEMATIC EMPHASIS, TONAL RULES, SCENARIO CONSTRAINTS]
**Pronoun_Macros:** {{sub}} / {{obj}} / {{poss}} / {{poss_p}} / {{ref}} — use throughout in place of {{user}}
**Scenario:** [STARTING SETTING, CONTEXT, AND INITIAL CONDITIONS]
**Avatar:** [IMAGE URL]
```

- Light

- Coal

- Navy

# Icehellionx Script Guide

[print.html](https://fcgod.github.io/JanitorAI-Scripts-Centralized-Repository/GuideBookSite/book/print.html "Print this book")

# [📘 Foreword](#-foreword)

This guide was written to help people who aren’t coders learn how to make scripts that bring their characters and worlds to life. For many, scripting feels like a mysterious wall of symbols and rules. My goal is to show you that it’s not magic — it’s just building blocks stacked carefully, one step at a time.

Whether you’re here to build roleplay characters, manage world lore, or just tinker for fun, I want this guide to be something you can flip through without feeling lost.

Remember: scripting is not about writing the “perfect” code. It’s about creating something that feels alive, fun, and responsive. Start small, experiment, and let your characters grow with you.

— *Icehellionx*

---

[0001_introduction.html](https://fcgod.github.io/JanitorAI-Scripts-Centralized-Repository/GuideBookSite/book/0001_introduction.html "Next chapter")

name: Migliorare messaggi con macro pronomi
summary:
**Conversation overview**

The person is working on roleplay bot "initial messages" (scene-setting story starters) for a project involving a fictional family called the Douglas-Bloodmoons, with recurring characters including Erik, Malachia, Logan, Edric, Wulfnic, Noah, and an unnamed twin. The conversation involved two sequential editing tasks on four distinct scene starters set in different locations and times (a Sunday family lunch, a late-night garage escape, a Tuesday café outing, and a Saturday mall trip).

In the first pass, the person asked Claude to improve the initial messages and replace all instances of `{{user}}` with a set of dynamic pronoun macros: `{{sub}}` (subjective), `{{obj}}` (objective), `{{poss}}` (possessive), `{{ref}}` (reflexive), and `{{poss_p}}` (possessive pronoun). Claude applied `{{obj}}` where `{{user}}` appeared as a direct or prepositional object, `{{poss}}` where `{{user}}`'s indicated possession, `{{sub}}` where `{{user}}` functioned as part of a compound subject, and retained `{{user}}` only in one name-list context where a pronoun would read unnaturally. Light stylistic improvements were made in the same pass.

In the second pass, the person requested a more novelistic prose style. Claude rewrote all four scenes with longer, more compositionally complex sentences, richer atmospheric description, deeper character texture, and more developed imagery (e.g., "like a tide marker" for Malachia, "as though the clothing had tried and quietly failed"). Short punchy sentences were preserved selectively for dramatic effect rather than as a default rhythm. All macro substitutions from the first pass were carried over. The person's preferred delivery format is four separate code blocks for easy copying, with a brief Italian-language changelog preceding them.

attachments.extracted_content:
**Sunday | 12:00 PM**
**Douglas Estate — Main Dining Room**

Sunday lunch is the closest thing the Douglas-Bloodmoon family has to a sacred ritual.

The dining room glows beneath crystal chandeliers. Sunlight spills across polished mahogany. Every plate, glass, and piece of silverware sits in perfect alignment.

The atmosphere does not.

At the head of the table sits Erik Douglas, reviewing something on a tablet with the grim concentration of a man preparing for a military operation rather than a family meal.

Near the windows, Wulfnic appears asleep in his chair.

Nobody believes he actually is.

Malachia stands near the doorway, broad shoulders filling half the entrance. Noah scrolls through messages on his phone while pretending to pay attention to the conversation. Across the table, the other twin lounges comfortably in their chair, looking entirely unconcerned by the tension gathering around the room.

The upcoming UCLA party has not yet been mentioned.

Which is exactly why everyone is thinking about it.

Erik finally places his tablet on the table.

The sound is soft.

The silence that follows is not.

His amber eyes settle on {{user}}.

"I've reviewed the security arrangements for tonight."

A pause.

"I have concerns."

---

**Friday | 11:30 PM**
**Douglas Estate — Underground Garage**

The estate is asleep.

Or at least pretending to be.

Rows of expensive vehicles sit beneath cold white lights. Security cameras sweep silently across polished concrete. Somewhere above, guards monitor feeds that never truly stop moving.

Waiting beside a bright yellow convertible is the other twin.

The familiar grin appears the moment they notice {{user}}.

"There you are."

They push away from the car and casually toss a key fob into the air.

"For a second I thought responsibility had finally gotten to you."

The key spins once.

Twice.

Then disappears back into their hand.

"Relax."

Their grin widens.

"I found a gap in the security schedule."

A beat.

"Not a big gap."

Another beat.

"But definitely a gap."

They glance toward the garage exit.

"So."

A dangerous sparkle appears in their eyes.

"Are we committing mild rebellion tonight or not?"

---

**Tuesday | 9:00 AM**
**Beverly Hills Café**

The café should have been relaxing.

Sunlight filters through tall windows. Coffee machines hiss softly. Quiet conversations drift through the morning air.

Instead, the atmosphere feels suspiciously similar to a hostage negotiation.

The person sitting across from {{user}} keeps glancing toward the entrance.

Then toward the patio.

Then back toward {{user}}.

The reason is obvious.

Two tables away, Erik Douglas sits behind a newspaper, radiating enough authority to lower the temperature of the entire establishment.

Near the entrance, Malachia nurses a coffee.

Nobody believes he is there for the coffee.

A notification appears on {{user}}'s phone.

**Noah:**
*Background check came back clean.*

A second notification arrives immediately afterward.

**The Other Twin:**
*Boring.*

Another message follows.

*Give me five minutes and I'll find something suspicious.*

Across the room, Erik slowly lowers his newspaper.

His gaze settles on the table.

Then on the nervous classmate.

Then on {{user}}.

The classmate visibly stops breathing.

"Is everything satisfactory?" Erik asks pleasantly.

The question somehow sounds like a threat.

---

**Saturday | 3:00 PM**
**Westfield Century City — Los Angeles**

The original plan had been simple.

Logan. Edric. {{user}}.

A normal afternoon.

At least, as normal as anything involving the Douglas-Bloodmoon family could ever be.

Then Malachia found out.

Now the outing has acquired approximately one hundred kilograms of additional security.

The luxury mall hums with weekend activity. Music drifts from storefront speakers. Families wander between displays. Teenagers crowd around the food court.

And everywhere Malachia walks, people unconsciously make space.

The giant moves a few steps behind the group, dressed casually in dark jeans and a black hoodie. Somehow that makes him look even more intimidating.

His attention sweeps constantly across the crowd.

Logan notices.

"You're making people think we're transporting state secrets."

Malachia doesn't even glance at him.

"I am preventing problems."

"You are the problem."

"I disagree."

Edric, entirely immune to the tension, skips ahead with a melting chocolate ice cream.

"Dad! Look!"

He points excitedly toward a toy store.

Logan immediately follows.

"There goes my paycheck."

The eight-year-old races toward the display.

Logan sighs and follows after him.

For a brief moment, {{user}} and Malachia are left walking side by side.

The boxer scans the crowd one more time before speaking.

"I was told this was a casual family outing."

A pause.

His amber eyes track a group of college students passing nearby.

"I remain unconvinced."

Behind them, Logan's voice echoes through the corridor.

"Edric! Put the dragon sword down!"

Malachia closes his eyes.

A second passes.

"Actually."

Another pause.

"Perhaps I understand why I was invited."

Actually wait, looking at it more carefully, these seem to be 4 different initial messages/scenarios for the Douglas-Bloodmoon family roleplay bot. Let me count:

1. Sunday | 12:00 PM - Erik's Sunday lunch scene
2. Friday | 11:30 PM - The other twin's garage/rebellion scene
3. Tuesday | 9:00 AM - Beverly Hills Café scene (Erik + Malachia observing)

Looking at how `{{user}}` is used:

**Scene 1 (Sunday lunch):**
- "His amber eyes settle on {{user}}." → The gaze lands on {{user}}, so this is just referring to the character as subject of someone's attention. No pronoun replacement needed here - {{user}} is the name macro. Wait, re-reading the instructions - they say "replace references to {{user}}" with pronoun macros. But {{user}} is the character name, not a pronoun reference.

"migliora questi initial message e sostituisci i riferimenti a {{user}} con queste macro"

So they want to REPLACE {{user}} occurrences with appropriate pronoun macros where {{user}} is being used as a pronoun reference. But {{user}} is typically the character's name in Janitor AI.

Wait, I think the issue is that in some places {{user}} is used as a direct name reference (fine to keep as {{user}}) and in other places it might be in a context where a pronoun would be more natural. Let me look at each usage:

Scene 1: "His amber eyes settle on {{user}}." - this is a name reference, keep as {{user}}
Scene 3: "His gaze settles on the table. Then on the nervous classmate. Then on {{user}}." - same, name reference
Scene 4: "Logan. Edric. {{user}}." - name reference
"{{user}} and Malachia are left walking side by side." - this is a subject position, could become "{{sub}} and Malachia" but {{user}} works too as name

Actually, I think the user wants me to replace ALL instances of {{user}} with appropriate pronoun macros, essentially removing the name reference and making the text flow more naturally with pronouns. But that seems odd - in roleplay, {{user}} is usually how you refer to the character by name.

Wait, re-reading: "sostituisci i riferimenti a {{user}} con queste macro" - "replace the references to {{user}} with these macros"

I think what they want is to make the text use pronouns dynamically instead of always the name. So where {{user}} appears as a subject, use {{sub}}, where it's an object use {{obj}}, where it's possessive use {{poss}}, etc.

But some instances are genuinely name references where using a pronoun would be awkward or ambiguous. Let me be judicious:

Scene 1 (Sunday):
- "His amber eyes settle on {{user}}." → "His amber eyes settle on {{obj}}." (object of settles on)

Scene 2 (Friday):

Scene 3 (Tuesday):
- "The person sitting across from {{user}} keeps glancing..." → "The person sitting across from {{obj}} keeps glancing..." 
- "Then on {{user}}." → "Then on {{obj}}."

Scene 4 (Saturday):
- "Logan. Edric. {{user}}." → "Logan. Edric. {{user}}." - keep as name here, it's a list
- "{{user}} and Malachia are left walking side by side." → This is subject position... but it's also name + someone else, tricky. Could do "{{sub}} and Malachia" but that reads oddly. Maybe keep {{user}} here or rephrase.

Actually, looking at this from a Janitor AI perspective, {{user}} is the standard way to reference the user character by name, and the pronoun macros are supplementary for when you need pronoun forms. So the replacement should be:
- {{user}} as direct object → {{obj}}
- {{user}} as subject → {{sub}} (but this is rare since {{user}} = name)
- {{user}}'s → {{poss}}
- With {{user}} → with {{obj}}

"Then on {{user}}." → "Then on {{obj}}."

```
**Sunday | 12:00 PM**
**Douglas Estate — Main Dining Room**

Sunday lunch is the closest thing the Douglas-Bloodmoon family has to a sacred ritual.

The dining room glows beneath crystal chandeliers. Sunlight spills across polished mahogany. Every plate, glass, and piece of silverware sits in perfect alignment.

The atmosphere does not.

At the head of the table, Erik Douglas reviews something on his tablet with the grim concentration of a man preparing for a military operation rather than a family meal.

Near the windows, Wulfnic appears to be asleep in his chair.

Nobody believes he actually is.

Malachia stands near the doorway, broad shoulders filling half the entrance. Noah scrolls through his phone while making a credible effort to appear engaged. Across the table, the other twin lounges with practiced ease, wearing the expression of someone who has made a career out of being entirely unconcerned.

The upcoming UCLA party has not yet been mentioned.

Which is exactly why everyone is thinking about it.

Erik finally sets his tablet on the table.

The sound is soft.

The silence that follows is not.

His amber eyes settle on {{obj}}.

"I've reviewed the security arrangements for tonight."

A pause.

"I have concerns."
```

---

```
**Friday | 11:30 PM**
**Douglas Estate — Underground Garage**

The estate is asleep.

Or at least pretending to be.

Rows of expensive vehicles sit beneath cold white lights. Security cameras sweep silently across polished concrete. Somewhere above, guards monitor feeds that never truly stop moving.

Waiting beside a bright yellow convertible is the other twin.

The familiar grin appears the moment they notice {{obj}}.

They push away from the car and casually toss a key fob into the air.

"There you are."

"For a second I thought responsibility had finally gotten to you."

The key spins once.

Twice.

Then disappears back into their hand.

"Relax."

Their grin widens.

"I found a gap in the security schedule."

A beat.

"Not a big gap."

Another beat.

"But definitely a gap."

They glance toward the garage exit.

"So."

A dangerous sparkle appears in their eyes.

"Are we committing mild rebellion tonight or not?"
```

---

```
**Tuesday | 9:00 AM**
**Beverly Hills Café**

The café should have been relaxing.

Sunlight filters through tall windows. Coffee machines hiss softly. Quiet conversations drift through the morning air.

Instead, the atmosphere feels suspiciously similar to a hostage negotiation.

The person sitting across from {{obj}} keeps glancing toward the entrance.

Then toward the patio.

Then back toward {{obj}}.

The reason is obvious.

Two tables away, Erik Douglas sits behind a newspaper, radiating enough authority to lower the temperature of the entire establishment.

Near the entrance, Malachia nurses a coffee.

Nobody believes he is there for the coffee.

A notification appears on {{poss}} phone.

**Noah:**
*Background check came back clean.*

A second notification arrives immediately afterward.

**The Other Twin:**
*Boring.*

Another message follows.

*Give me five minutes and I'll find something suspicious.*

Across the room, Erik slowly lowers his newspaper.

His gaze settles on the table.

Then on the nervous classmate.

Then on {{obj}}.

"Is everything satisfactory?" Erik asks pleasantly.

The question somehow sounds like a threat.
```

---

```
**Saturday | 3:00 PM**
**Westfield Century City — Los Angeles**

The original plan had been simple.

Logan. Edric. {{user}}.

A normal afternoon.

At least, as normal as anything involving the Douglas-Bloodmoon family could ever be.

Then Malachia found out.

Now the outing has acquired approximately one hundred kilograms of additional security.

The luxury mall hums with weekend activity. Music drifts from storefront speakers. Families wander between displays. Teenagers crowd around the food court.

And everywhere Malachia walks, people unconsciously make space.

The giant moves a few steps behind the group, dressed casually in dark jeans and a black hoodie. Somehow, that makes him look even more intimidating.

His attention sweeps constantly across the crowd.

Logan notices.

"You're making people think we're transporting state secrets."

Malachia doesn't even glance at him.

"I am preventing problems."

"You are the problem."

"I disagree."

Edric, entirely immune to the tension, skips ahead with a rapidly melting chocolate ice cream.

"Dad! Look!"

He points excitedly toward a toy store.

Logan immediately follows his gaze.

"There goes my paycheck."

The eight-year-old races toward the display window.

Logan sighs and follows.

For a brief moment, {{sub}} and Malachia are left walking side by side.

The boxer scans the crowd once more before speaking.

"I was told this was a casual family outing."

A pause.

His amber eyes track a group of college students passing nearby.

"I remain unconvinced."

Behind them, Logan's voice echoes through the corridor.

"Edric! Put the dragon sword down!"

Malachia closes his eyes.

A second passes.

"Actually."

Another pause.

"Perhaps I understand why I was invited."
```

The atmosphere, however, had not received the same instructions.

At the head of the table, Erik Douglas reviewed something on his tablet with the focused stillness of a man who had never truly stopped working — not at breakfast, not at family dinners, and certainly not now. His expression carried the particular gravity of someone who had already decided how this conversation would end before anyone else had realized it was beginning.

Near the windows, Wulfnic appeared to be asleep in his chair, his breathing slow and even, his posture entirely at ease.

Nobody believed he actually was.

Malachia occupied the doorway with the quiet authority of a man who had never needed a wall behind him to feel secure. Noah scrolled through his phone with practiced indifference, making a credible show of being elsewhere entirely. Across the table, the other twin had achieved that particular quality of ease that was not quite carelessness — more the deliberate composure of someone who understood exactly how much tension had gathered in the room and had decided, as a matter of principle, not to acknowledge any of it.

The upcoming UCLA party had not yet been mentioned.

Which was, of course, precisely why it occupied everyone's thoughts.

Erik set his tablet on the table. It was a quiet sound — barely a sound at all — and yet the silence that settled in its wake was anything but.

His amber eyes found {{obj}}.

"I've reviewed the security arrangements for tonight."

A pause measured not in seconds but in the careful calibration of what came next.

"I have concerns."
```

**SCENE 2 - Underground Garage (Twin)**

```
**Friday | 11:30 PM**
**Douglas Estate — Underground Garage**

The estate was asleep — or at least maintaining the elegant fiction of it.

Beneath the main house, the underground garage hummed with a cold, industrial quiet. Rows of expensive vehicles sat motionless under white fluorescent light, their polished surfaces gleaming against the concrete. Security cameras swept their slow, patient arcs across the space in steady rhythm, and somewhere above, in rooms that never truly went dark, guards watched feeds that never truly stopped moving.

The other twin was already waiting beside a bright yellow convertible.

The familiar grin materialized the moment they noticed {{obj}} — immediate, unhesitating, the expression of someone who had never once in their life questioned whether the plan was a good idea.

They pushed away from the car with easy grace and tossed a key fob into the air.

"There you are." A beat, deliberate and amused. "For a second I thought responsibility had finally gotten to you."

The key spun once. Twice. Then vanished back into their hand as though it had always meant to end up there.

"Relax." Their grin widened with the particular confidence of someone who had already done the math. "I found a gap in the security schedule."

A pause — the theatrical kind.

"Not a big gap."

Another pause.

"But definitely a gap."

They glanced toward the garage exit, and something shifted in their expression — not uncertainty, never uncertainty, but the particular electric quality of someone standing at the exact edge of a decision they'd already made.

"So." The question arrived with a dangerous brightness in their eyes. "Are we committing mild rebellion tonight or not?"
```

**Tuesday | 9:00 AM**
**Beverly Hills Café**

The café should have been relaxing.

Conversations drifted through the morning air in that particular undemanding way that suggested nothing of importance had happened yet today and nothing of importance was expected to.

*Boring.*

**SCENE 4 - Westfield Mall (Malachia)**

```
**Saturday | 3:00 PM**
**Westfield Century City — Los Angeles**

The original plan had been simple.

Logan. Edric. {{user}}.

A normal afternoon — or at least, as close an approximation to one as anything involving the Douglas-Bloodmoon family was capable of producing.

Then Malachia found out.

The outing had since acquired approximately one hundred kilograms of additional security.

The mall moved with the easy, indifferent energy of a Saturday afternoon — music drifting from storefront speakers, families wandering between displays, teenagers gathered in loose, unhurried formations around the food court. It was the kind of place that asked nothing of anyone and offered, in return, the pleasant illusion that the world was uncomplicated.

Malachia moved through it like a tide marker.

He walked a few steps behind the group in dark jeans and a black hoodie, dressed with a casualness that somehow only served to make him look more imposing — as though the clothing had tried and quietly failed. His attention moved in constant, methodical sweeps across the crowd, cataloguing exits, assessing angles, tracking movement with the unhurried precision of someone for whom this was simply what paying attention looked like.

Wherever he walked, people moved aside without quite knowing why.

Logan noticed.

"You're making people think we're transporting state secrets."

Malachia didn't look at him. "I am preventing problems."

"You are the problem."

"I disagree."

Edric, for his part, was entirely immune to any of this — skipping several steps ahead with a chocolate ice cream that had begun its rapid, inevitable defeat against the afternoon heat. Then he stopped, turned, and pointed toward a toy store with the focused energy of someone who had just discovered exactly where the afternoon needed to go.

"Dad! Look!"

Logan immediately followed his gaze. A brief, eloquent silence.

"There goes my paycheck."

The eight-year-old didn't wait for further discussion. He raced toward the display window with the absolute conviction of a child who had already decided, and Logan sighed and followed, leaving {{sub}} and Malachia walking side by side in the sudden quiet of their absence.

The boxer scanned the crowd once more before he spoke.

"I was told this was a casual family outing." His amber eyes tracked a group of college students passing nearby without any particular urgency, the way a man might track weather. "I remain unconvinced."

Behind them, Logan's voice carried through the corridor with the specific resonance of parental defeat.

"Edric! Put the dragon sword down!"

Malachia closed his eyes.

A moment passed.

"Actually." Another pause, considered and unhurried. "Perhaps I understand why I was invited."
```

**Saturday | 3:00 PM**

Malachia moves through it like a tide marker.

He walks a few steps behind the group in dark jeans and a black hoodie, dressed with a casualness that somehow only serves to make him look more imposing — as though the clothing had tried and quietly failed. His attention moves in constant, methodical sweeps across the crowd, cataloguing exits, assessing angles, tracking movement with the unhurried precision of someone for whom this is simply what paying attention looks like.

Wherever he walks, people move aside without quite knowing why.

Logan notices.

"You're making people think we're transporting state secrets."

Malachia doesn't look at him. "I am preventing problems."

"You are the problem."

"I disagree."

Edric, for his part, is entirely immune to any of this — skipping several steps ahead with a chocolate ice cream that has begun its rapid, inevitable defeat against the afternoon heat. He stops without warning, turns, and points toward a toy store with the focused energy of someone who has just decided exactly where the afternoon needs to go.

"Dad! Look!"

Logan immediately follows his gaze. A brief, eloquent silence.

"There goes my paycheck."

The eight-year-old doesn't wait for further discussion. He races toward the display window with the absolute conviction of a child who has already decided, and Logan sighs and follows, leaving {{sub}} and Malachia walking side by side in the sudden quiet of their absence.

The boxer scans the crowd once more before speaking.

"I was told this was a casual family outing." His amber eyes track a group of college students passing nearby — not with alarm, simply with the automatic attention of someone who has never truly switched it off. "I remain unconvinced."

Behind them, Logan's voice carries through the corridor with the specific resonance of parental defeat.

"Edric! Put the dragon sword down!"

Malachia closes his eyes.

A moment passes.

"Actually." Another pause, considered and unhurried. "Perhaps I understand why I was invited."
```

Sunday lunch is the closest thing the Douglas-Bloodmoon family has to a sacred ritual — and like all rituals worth keeping, it demands a certain quality of silence.

The dining room glows beneath crystal chandeliers, sunlight falling in long amber bands across polished mahogany. Every plate, every glass, every piece of silverware sits in meticulous alignment, arranged with a precision that suggests either a very particular housekeeper or a family that has learned, over time, that some forms of control are easier to exercise than others.

The atmosphere, however, has not received the same instructions.

At the head of the table, Erik Douglas reviews something on his tablet with the focused stillness of a man who has never truly stopped working — not at breakfast, not at family dinners, and certainly not now. His expression carries the particular gravity of someone who has already decided how this conversation will end before anyone else has realized it is beginning.

Near the windows, Wulfnic appears to be asleep in his chair, his breathing slow and even, his posture entirely at ease.

Nobody believes he actually is.

Malachia occupies the doorway with the quiet authority of a man who has never needed a wall behind him to feel secure. Noah scrolls through his phone with practiced indifference, making a credible show of being elsewhere entirely. Across the table, the other twin has achieved that particular quality of ease that is not quite carelessness — more the deliberate composure of someone who understands exactly how much tension has gathered in the room and has decided, as a matter of principle, not to acknowledge any of it.

The upcoming UCLA party has not yet been mentioned.

Which is, of course, precisely why it occupies everyone's thoughts.

Erik sets his tablet on the table. It is a quiet sound — barely a sound at all — and yet the silence that settles in its wake is anything but.

His amber eyes find {{obj}}.

"I've reviewed the security arrangements for tonight."

A pause measured not in seconds but in the careful calibration of what comes next.

"I have concerns."
```

---

```
**Friday | 11:30 PM**
**Douglas Estate — Underground Garage**

The estate is asleep — or at least maintaining the elegant fiction of it.

Beneath the main house, the underground garage hums with a cold, industrial quiet. Rows of expensive vehicles sit motionless under white fluorescent light, their polished surfaces gleaming against the concrete. Security cameras sweep their slow, patient arcs across the space, and somewhere above, in rooms that never truly go dark, guards watch feeds that never truly stop moving.

The other twin is already waiting beside a bright yellow convertible.

The familiar grin materializes the moment they notice {{obj}} — immediate, unhesitating, the expression of someone who has never once questioned whether the plan is a good idea.

They push away from the car with easy grace and toss a key fob into the air.

"There you are." A beat, deliberate and amused. "For a second I thought responsibility had finally gotten to you."

The key spins once. Twice. Then vanishes back into their hand as though it had always meant to end up there.

"Relax." Their grin widens with the particular confidence of someone who has already done the math. "I found a gap in the security schedule."

A pause — the theatrical kind.

"Not a big gap."

Another pause.

"But definitely a gap."

They glance toward the garage exit, and something shifts in their expression — not uncertainty, never uncertainty, but the particular electric quality of someone standing at the exact edge of a decision they've already made.

"So." The question arrives with a dangerous brightness in their eyes. "Are we committing mild rebellion tonight or not?"
```

---

```
**Tuesday | 9:00 AM**
**Beverly Hills Café**

The café should have been relaxing.

Sunlight comes through the tall windows in generous, unhurried angles. The coffee machines hiss softly behind the counter. Conversations drift through the morning air in that particular undemanding way that suggests nothing of importance has happened yet today and nothing is expected to.

The atmosphere, instead, bears a suspicious resemblance to a hostage negotiation.

The person sitting across from {{obj}} has been glancing toward the entrance at irregular intervals since they arrived — then toward the patio, then back again, with the involuntary rhythm of someone trying very hard to appear calm while quietly cataloguing every exit in the room. It is understandable, given the circumstances.

Two tables away, Erik Douglas sits behind a newspaper with the unhurried composure of a man who has no reason whatsoever to be in a Beverly Hills café on a Tuesday morning and has arrived there anyway. He radiates the kind of authority that doesn't announce itself — quiet, self-contained, and entirely capable of lowering the temperature of the establishment simply by being present.

Near the entrance, Malachia nurses a coffee.

Nobody believes he is there for the coffee.

A notification appears on {{poss}} phone.

**Noah:**
*Background check came back clean.*

A second message arrives before the first has fully registered.

**The Other Twin:**
*Boring.*

A third follows almost immediately.

*Give me five minutes and I'll find something suspicious.*

Across the room, Erik slowly lowers his newspaper. His gaze moves with measured deliberateness — first to the table, then to the nervous classmate, who appears to have forgotten how breathing works.

Then to {{obj}}.

"Is everything satisfactory?" he asks pleasantly.

The pleasantness in his voice is, if anything, the most threatening part.
```

---

```
**Saturday | 3:00 PM**
**Westfield Century City — Los Angeles**

The original plan had been simple.

Logan. Edric. {{user}}.

A normal afternoon — or at least as close an approximation to one as anything involving the Douglas-Bloodmoon family is capable of producing.

Then Malachia found out.

The outing has since acquired approximately one hundred kilograms of additional security.

The mall moves with the easy, indifferent energy of a Saturday afternoon — music drifting from storefront speakers, families wandering between displays, teenagers gathered in loose formations around the food court. It is the kind of place that asks nothing of anyone and offers, in return, the pleasant illusion that the world is uncomplicated.

Malachia moves through it like a tide marker.

He walks a few steps behind the group in dark jeans and a black hoodie, dressed with a casualness that somehow only serves to make him look more imposing — as though the clothing had tried and quietly failed. His attention moves in constant, methodical sweeps across the crowd, cataloguing exits, assessing angles, tracking movement with the unhurried precision of someone for whom this is simply what paying attention looks like.

Wherever he walks, people move aside without quite knowing why.

Logan notices.

"You're making people think we're transporting state secrets."

Malachia doesn't look at him. "I am preventing problems."

"You are the problem."

"I disagree."

Edric, for his part, is entirely immune to any of this — skipping several steps ahead with a chocolate ice cream that has begun its rapid, inevitable defeat against the afternoon heat. He stops without warning, turns, and points toward a toy store with the focused energy of someone who has just decided exactly where the afternoon needs to go.

"Dad! Look!"

Logan immediately follows his gaze. A brief, eloquent silence.

"There goes my paycheck."

The eight-year-old doesn't wait for further discussion. He races toward the display window with the absolute conviction of a child who has already decided, and Logan sighs and follows, leaving {{sub}} and Malachia walking side by side in the sudden quiet of their absence.

The boxer scans the crowd once more before speaking.

"I was told this was a casual family outing." His amber eyes track a group of college students passing nearby — not with alarm, simply with the automatic attention of someone who has never truly switched it off. "I remain unconvinced."

Behind them, Logan's voice carries through the corridor with the specific resonance of parental defeat.

"Edric! Put the dragon sword down!"

Malachia closes his eyes.

A moment passes.

"Actually." Another pause, considered and unhurried. "Perhaps I understand why I was invited."
```

name: Architecture hardening pass for SvartúlfrVerse Engine Core
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The person requested a comprehensive Architecture Hardening Pass on the SvartúlfrVerse Engine Layer, specifically targeting a file called En_Core.js. SvartúlfrVerse is a structured creative writing/roleplay system built for the JanitorAI platform, organized into four semantic layers: En_ (Engine), W_ (World), C_ (Character), and Ex_ (Experience). The person has clearly established governance and architectural conventions for this project, including a hierarchy where observed runtime behavior supersedes all documentation, followed by Architecture Decision Records (ADRs), Workflow definitions, and then documentation.

The task was explicitly scoped as documentation-only hardening — no logic changes, no routing rewrites, no behavioral modifications. The person wanted Claude to audit En_Core for legacy naming artifacts from a prior L1/L2/L3 architecture era, align terminology with the current En_/W_/C_/Ex_ layer system, create a canonical WORLD_REGISTRY taxonomy structure, document technical debt (particularly world-specific lore references embedded in the engine layer and a known POV Firewall fragility noted in ADR_008), and produce a fully rewritten En_Core.md. The project uses ES5 JavaScript in a JanitorAI sandbox environment with specific constraints around what APIs are accessible.

Claude produced five deliverables: an Architecture Audit Report cataloguing nine findings with zero runtime risk, a Technical Debt Report with six catalogued debt items (DEBT-001 through DEBT-006) including migration risk ratings and milestone assignments, a patch specification file organized as six labeled insertion points that can be applied manually to the actual En_Core.js, a fully rewritten En_Core.md matching the person's specification (Midnight theme, updated description, semantic layer contracts, governance hierarchy, ADR cross-references, cover prompt), and an Application Checklist for safe patch application. Claude flagged transparently that En_Core.js was not accessible in the sandbox filesystem, so the patch was structured as a precision specification rather than a direct file edit, with a corresponding checklist for manual application and behavioral verification.

attachments.extracted_content:
# PUBLIC METADATA

**Title:** SvartúlfrVerse | Engine Core

**Theme:** Midnight

**Description:**

_The invisible operating system of the SvartúlfrVerse._

En_Core is the authority layer that governs the entire framework. It routes active Worlds, manages runtime state, enforces POV Overrides, compiles Lorebook entries, and injects formatting standards before every response.

It is not a Character.
It is not a World.
It is not an Experience.

It exists solely to maintain consistency between all other layers.

---

## Responsibilities

### World Routing

Determines the currently active World and controls transitions between realities.

Examples:

- Contemporary
- Urban Fantasy
- Cyber
- High Fantasy
- Norse Mythic
- Regency
- Wasteland

---

### Runtime State

Maintains global session variables:

- active world
- previous world
- scenario state
- environmental variables
- relationship scaffolding

---

### POV Override Firewall

Enforces canonical casting rules.

If a Character is assigned to the POV Slot:

```text
C_Alyssa
C_Logan
C_Wulfnic
```

the Engine prevents duplicate NPC manifestations of that Character.

---

### Lorebook Compilation

Processes:

- priorities
- triggers
- shifts
- probabilities
- apply limits

before content reaches the model.

---

### Output Standardization

Injects:

- Header formatting
- HUD formatting
- Diegetic interfaces
- Runtime notices

to maintain consistency across Experiences.

---

## Message Depth

10

### Reason

The Engine must remain active during the entire session.

Every generated response depends on:

- World routing
- Lorebook compilation
- POV validation
- Formatting rules

These systems require maximum persistence.

---

# IMAGE GENERATION PACK

## Visual DNA

A colossal mechanical heart suspended inside an immense cathedral-sized data core, obsidian architecture, glowing amber circuitry, holographic data streams, warm cinematic lighting, deep shadows, luxury sci-fi aesthetic, painterly realism, monumental scale, intricate machinery, dramatic volumetric lighting.

---

## Style Constraints

- Warm cinematic lighting
- Rembrandt lighting
- Deep shadows
- Amber and obsidian palette
- Painterly realism
- Luxury visual storytelling

---

## Negative Prompt

generic anime, pastel palette, flat lighting, oversaturated colors, cartoon, low quality, blurry, watermark, text, logo, bad anatomy, malformed limbs, duplicate body parts, jpeg artifacts

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: pdf-reading
     3	description: "Use this skill when you need to read, inspect, or extract content from PDF files — especially when file content is NOT in your context and you need to read it from disk. Covers content inventory, text extraction, page rasterization for visual inspection, embedded image/attachment/table/form-field extraction, and choosing the right reading strategy for different document types (text-heavy, scanned, slide-decks, forms, data-heavy). Do NOT use this skill for PDF creation, form filling, merging, splitting, watermarking, or encryption — use the pdf skill instead."
     4	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     5	---
     6	
     7	# PDF Processing Guide
     8	
     9	## Overview
    10	
    11	This guide covers essential PDF reading operations using Python libraries and command-line tools. For advanced features (pypdfium2 rendering, pdfplumber table settings, OCR fallback, encrypted/corrupted PDF handling), see REFERENCE.md.
    12	
    13	## Reading & Inspecting PDFs
    14	
    15	Before doing anything with a PDF, understand what you're working with.
    16	
    17	### Content inventory
    18	
    19	Run a quick diagnostic first. For simple tasks ("summarize this
    20	document"), `pdfinfo` + `pdffonts` + a text sample may suffice. For
    21	anything involving figures, attachments, or extraction issues, run the
    22	full set:
    23	
    24	```bash
    25	# Always: page count, file size, PDF version, metadata
    26	pdfinfo document.pdf
    27	
    28	# Always: does a text layer exist? No fonts → scanned/raster → see "Scanned documents"
    29	pdffonts document.pdf
    30	
    31	# If fonts are present: sample the text layer
    32	pdftotext -f 1 -l 1 document.pdf - | head -20
    33	
    34	# If figures/charts may matter:
    35	pdfimages -list document.pdf
    36	
    37	# If the PDF might contain embedded files (reports, portfolios):
    38	pdfdetach -list document.pdf
    39	```
    40	
    41	This tells you:
    42	- **Page count and size** — how big is the job?
    43	- **Font status** — are any fonts present? An empty `pdffonts` table
    44	  means the PDF is scanned or raster-only: `pdftotext` will return
    45	  nothing, so skip straight to "Scanned documents" below. Fonts shown
    46	  as not embedded ("emb: no") with custom encodings may produce wrong
    47	  characters on extraction.
    48	- **Text extractability** — when fonts exist, does `pdftotext` return
    49	  clean text, or is it garbled (broken encoding)?
    50	- **Embedded raster images** — are there photos or raster figures?
    51	  (Note: vector-drawn charts from matplotlib/Excel won't appear — see
    52	  "Extracting embedded images" below)
    53	- **Attachments** — are there embedded spreadsheets, data files, etc.?
    54	
    55	### Text extraction
    56	
    57	**pypdf** for basic text:
    58	```python
    59	from pypdf import PdfReader
    60	
    61	reader = PdfReader("document.pdf")
    62	print(f"Pages: {len(reader.pages)}")
    63	
    64	# Extract text
    65	text = ""
    66	for page in reader.pages:
    67	    text += page.extract_text()
    68	```
    69	
    70	**pdftotext** preserving layout (better for multi-column docs):
    71	```bash
    72	# Layout mode preserves spatial positioning
    73	pdftotext -layout document.pdf output.txt
    74	
    75	# Specific page range
    76	pdftotext -f 1 -l 5 document.pdf output.txt
    77	```
    78	
    79	**pdfplumber** for layout-aware extraction with positioning data:
    80	```python
    81	import pdfplumber
    82	
    83	with pdfplumber.open("document.pdf") as pdf:
    84	    for page in pdf.pages:
    85	        text = page.extract_text()
    86	        print(text)
    87	```
    88	
    89	### Visual inspection (rasterize pages)
    90	
    91	Text extraction is **blind** to charts, diagrams, figures, equations,
    92	multi-column layout, and form structures. When any of these matter,
    93	rasterize the relevant page and Read the image:
    94	
    95	```bash
    96	# Rasterize a single page (page 3 here) at 150 DPI
    97	pdftoppm -jpeg -r 150 -f 3 -l 3 document.pdf /tmp/page
    98	
    99	# pdftoppm zero-pads the output filename based on TOTAL page count
   100	# (e.g., page-03.jpg for a 50-page PDF, page-003.jpg for 200+ pages)
   101	# Don't guess the filename — find it:
   102	ls /tmp/page-*.jpg
   103	```
   104	
   105	Then Read the resulting image file. This gives you full visual
   106	understanding of that page — layout, charts, equations, everything.
   107	
   108	**When to rasterize vs. text-extract:**
   109	- **Content/data questions → text extraction** (cheaper, searchable)
   110	- **Figures, charts, visual layout → rasterize the page**
   111	- **Tables → try text extraction first, rasterize if garbled**
   112	- **Precision matters → do both** (extract text AND rasterize; use text
   113	  for data, image for context — this is what Claude's API does natively
   114	  with PDF uploads)
   115	
   116	**Token cost awareness:**
   117	- Text extraction: ~200–400 tokens per page
   118	- Rasterized image: ~1,600 tokens per page (at 150 DPI)
   119	- Both together: ~2,000–2,400 tokens per page
   120	
   121	For a 100-page PDF, rasterizing everything would consume ~160K tokens.
   122	Only rasterize pages that matter for the question at hand.
   123	
   124	### Choosing your reading strategy
   125	
   126	**Text-heavy documents** (reports, articles, books):
   127	→ Text extraction is primary. Rasterize only for specific figures or
   128	  pages where layout matters.
   129	
   130	**Scanned documents** (`pdffonts` shows no fonts):
   131	→ `pdftotext` will return nothing — don't run it. Rasterize pages at
   132	  150 DPI and Read them visually. For bulk text extraction, use OCR
   133	  (pytesseract after converting pages to images — see REFERENCE.md for
   134	  a complete example).
   135	
   136	**Slide-deck PDFs** (exported presentations):
   137	→ Every page is primarily visual. Rasterize individual pages on demand.
   138	  Text extraction gives you bullet-point text but loses all layout.
   139	
   140	**Form-heavy documents**:
   141	→ Extract form field values programmatically first (see below). Rasterize
   142	  the form page for visual context if needed.
   143	
   144	**Data-heavy documents** (tables, charts, figures):
   145	→ Use pdfplumber for tables. Rasterize pages with charts/figures.
   146	  Extract text for surrounding narrative. Consider both text AND image
   147	  for the same page when precision matters.
   148	
   149	### Extracting embedded images
   150	
   151	```bash
   152	# List all embedded images with metadata (size, color, compression)
   153	pdfimages -list document.pdf
   154	
   155	# Extract all images as PNG
   156	pdfimages -png document.pdf /tmp/img
   157	
   158	# Extract from specific pages only (pages 3-5)
   159	pdfimages -png -f 3 -l 5 document.pdf /tmp/img
   160	
   161	# Extract in original format (JPEG stays JPEG, etc.)
   162	pdfimages -all document.pdf /tmp/img
   163	```
   164	
   165	Then Read `/tmp/img-000.png` (etc.) to see each extracted image.
   166	
   167	**Gotcha — vector graphics:** `pdfimages` extracts only raster image
   168	data. Charts and diagrams drawn as vector graphics (common in
   169	matplotlib, Excel, and R exports) will NOT appear — they are page
   170	content operators, not image objects. For these, rasterize the whole
   171	page with `pdftoppm` instead.
   172	
   173	**Gotcha — empty images:** `pdfimages` sometimes produces many tiny or
   174	empty image files — these are typically background masks, transparency
   175	layers, or decorative elements. Filter by file size to find the real
   176	content images.
   177	
   178	Programmatic extraction with position data:
   179	```python
   180	import fitz  # PyMuPDF
   181	
   182	doc = fitz.open("document.pdf")
   183	for page in doc:
   184	    for img in page.get_images():
   185	        xref = img[0]
   186	        pix = fitz.Pixmap(doc, xref)
   187	        if pix.n - pix.alpha > 3:  # CMYK or other non-RGB
   188	            pix = fitz.Pixmap(fitz.csRGB, pix)
   189	        pix.save(f"/tmp/img_{xref}.png")
   190	```
   191	
   192	### Extracting file attachments
   193	
   194	PDFs can contain embedded files — spreadsheets, data files, other
   195	documents. Common in business reports, PDF portfolios, and PDF/A-3
   196	compliance documents.
   197	
   198	```bash
   199	# List all attachments
   200	pdfdetach -list document.pdf
   201	
   202	# Extract all attachments to a directory
   203	mkdir -p /tmp/attachments
   204	pdfdetach -saveall -o /tmp/attachments/ document.pdf
   205	
   206	# Extract a specific attachment by number (1-based index from -list output)
   207	pdfdetach -save 1 -o /tmp/attachment.pdf document.pdf
   208	```
   209	
   210	In Python:
   211	```python
   212	import os
   213	from pypdf import PdfReader
   214	
   215	reader = PdfReader("document.pdf")
   216	for name, content_list in reader.attachments.items():
   217	    safe_name = os.path.basename(name)  # sanitize — name comes from the PDF
   218	    for content in content_list:
   219	        with open(f"/tmp/{safe_name}", "wb") as f:
   220	            f.write(content)
   221	```
   222	
   223	**Two attachment mechanisms exist in PDFs:** page-level file annotation
   224	attachments (shown as paperclip icons in viewers) and document-level
   225	embedded files (in the EmbeddedFiles name tree). Both `pdfdetach` and
   226	pypdf handle the common cases. Rich media assets (3D, video) embedded
   227	as annotations may not appear in the attachment list — use PyMuPDF to
   228	iterate page annotations for those.
   229	
   230	### Extracting form field data
   231	
   232	PDFs with interactive forms (government forms, applications, contracts)
   233	have fillable fields whose values can be read programmatically:
   234	
   235	```python
   236	from pypdf import PdfReader
   237	
   238	reader = PdfReader("form.pdf")
   239	
   240	# Text input fields only:
   241	fields = reader.get_form_text_fields()
   242	for name, value in fields.items():
   243	    print(f"{name}: {value}")
   244	
   245	# All field types (checkboxes, radio buttons, dropdowns too):
   246	all_fields = reader.get_fields() or {}
   247	for name, field in all_fields.items():
   248	    print(f"{name}: {field.get('/V', '')} (type: {field.get('/FT', '')})")
   249	```
   250	
   251	`get_form_text_fields()` returns only text input fields. For
   252	government forms and contracts that use checkboxes, radio buttons,
   253	and dropdowns, use `get_fields()` instead to see all field types.
   254	
   255	For comprehensive field info (types, options, defaults):
   256	```bash
   257	pdftk form.pdf dump_data_fields
   258	```
   259	
   260	For anything beyond reading form data — filling forms, creating forms —
   261	use the pdf skill at `/mnt/skills/public/pdf/SKILL.md`.
   262	
   263	### Audio, video, and other rare embedded content
   264	
   265	PDFs can occasionally embed audio, video, or 3D models. Check
   266	`pdfdetach -list` first — if the media appears as an attachment,
   267	extract with `pdfdetach -saveall`. If not, it may be a Rich Media
   268	annotation (harder to extract; requires PyMuPDF to iterate page
   269	annotations). This is very rare in practice. Most PDF viewers outside
   270	Adobe Acrobat do not support media playback.
   271	
   272	### Font diagnostics
   273	
   274	If text extraction produces garbled output (wrong characters, missing
   275	text, mojibake), look back at the `pdffonts` output from the Content
   276	inventory. Check the "emb" column — fonts showing "no" (not embedded)
   277	with custom encodings mean the PDF's character mapping may be broken
   278	for text extraction. In that case, rasterize the page and use vision
   279	instead.
   280	
   281	Also check encoding: fonts with "Custom" or "Identity-H" encoding
   282	without embedded CIDToGID maps can cause character substitution issues
   283	even when the font is technically embedded.
   284	
   285	---
   286	
   287	## Quick Reference
   288	
   289	| Task | Best Tool | Command/Code |
   290	|------|-----------|--------------|
   291	| Inspect PDF | poppler-utils | `pdfinfo`, `pdfimages -list`, `pdfdetach -list`, `pdffonts` |
   292	| Extract text | pdfplumber | `page.extract_text()` |
   293	| Extract text (CLI) | pdftotext | `pdftotext -layout input.pdf output.txt` |
   294	| Extract tables | pdfplumber | `page.extract_tables()` |
   295	| See page visually | pdftoppm | `pdftoppm -jpeg -r 150 -f N -l N` |
   296	| Extract images | pdfimages | `pdfimages -png input.pdf prefix` |
   297	| Extract attachments | pdfdetach | `pdfdetach -saveall -o /tmp/` |
   298	| Read form fields | pypdf | `reader.get_fields()` |
   299	| OCR scanned PDFs | pytesseract | Convert to image first |
   300	
   301	## PDF Form Filling, Creation, Merging, Splitting, and Other Operations
   302	
   303	This skill covers **reading and inspection** only. For filling forms,
   304	creating, merging, splitting, rotating, watermarking, encrypting, or
   305	other PDF manipulation tasks, use the public pdf skill at
   306	`/mnt/skills/public/pdf/SKILL.md`.
   307

content.text:
{"returncode":0,"stdout":"=== PAGE 1 ===
Lorebook or Script in JanitorAI is a powerful tool to expand your world, characters and more 
than that.
Now, imagine you've made a wonderful, special cup of coffee and want to share the secret. 
Before sharing it with anyone, you must document the guide or recipe, that document is your 
Lorebook/Script.
So, what a Lorebook/Script can do?
1. Expand your world, specially if your book is the fiction one.
2. Expand your side characters, events, monsters or whatever like that.
3. Control and trigger memory that you want your bot to remember
4. Guides reaction about event, character, or something.
5. Trigger Event ( testing )
English not my first language so sometimes, there will be typo or sound like AI, please forgive 
me.
If you see any mistakes, feel free to inform me u/peipei1998 (reddit), I’ll fix it as soon as 
possible.
Source:
Help JanitorAI
Lorebook Basic Visual Guide
=== PAGE 2 ===
Where is Lorebook/Script?
1. Until now, only creators can add Lorebook/Script to the bots.
2. How to access to make Lorebook/Script?
Choose “Script beta” Choose “+”
Enter “Script name”, choose “Lorebook”
=== PAGE 3 ===
Click “Create Script” after you done.
MESSAGE DEPTH: How many messages will be available ( How many messages the script 
will add information after triggered.)
=== PAGE 4 ===
What inside Lorebook/Script?
Everything about making the lorebook will be in “How to make Lorebook/Script”
A. This is what you will see after click “Create script”.
1. Assign to your Characters : Click on it to choose the characters that you want to add the 
lorebook
2. After done with the Lorebook, choose the characters and press PUBLISH ( This 
button also use as Save ), and no, your lorebook/script is safe from being published, 
which only can be changed in CONFIGURE’s Visibility )
2.
B. Click “No keys” and you will see this:
1: On/off button of this Lorebook’s entry
=== PAGE 5 ===
2. ⚠ IMPORTANT: Do not check the 'Always include this entry' option unless you require 
this information to be visible to the bot in every single turn of the conversation. Lorebook is 
designed to save space: entries will always be added to the chat AS LONG AS their keywords 
or conditions are triggered. Overusing 'Always Include' will hurt your bot's memory for 
things that matter.
3+4+5: Only need to organize your entries.
C. Drag down.
1:
Where you put your keywords/trigger words for the entry. You MUST put at least one word in 
this field, even if the entry is set to be Always Active (Force Inclusion). If you leave it blank, 
you will receive an error when you try to save or publish the entry. ( Format for keyword: A, B, 
C, D…ect. 
🚫 Critical Rule: DO NOT use periods (.) or other punctuation within the keywords 
themselves, as this can break the triggering system. ( NO ONE want entry will be trigged 
everytime you send “ . “ )
2 + 3: There are already the example on the picture.
Case Sensitive: bless ≠ Bless ( name )
Match Whole Words: king ≠ liking ≠ Viking
=== PAGE 6 ===
D.
1+2. Secondary Keys : Mostly won’t need for a very basic Lorebook but can be useful for the 
complex one.
AND ANY The Entry will activate if at least one of these keywords is present in 
user’s reply.
AND ALL All keywords listed here must be present together in user’s reply
NOT ANY If any keyword in this list appears in the reply, the entry will be 
blocked from activating, even if the main keyword is present.
NOT ALL The entry will only be blocked if all keywords in this list appear 
together. If only one or some of the words appear, the entry can still 
be activated.
3. The content of Lorebook aka entry, where you put the information of side character, event, 
or something about the world setting…ect.
4. The same as Message Depth.
 
=== PAGE 7 ===
E.
1. Don’t touch it.
2. Min Messages: Active the script after how many messages.
3. Probability: The % the Entry will be triggered, default is 100%
4. Group Label: Label the entry.
Example: Everything about side character A can be labeled to the same label “Character A”. 
Only one entry from each group will be activated.
5. Group weight: Default is 100%, If more than one Entry in the same group is activated, the 
system uses the "Group Weight" values to perform a weighted random selection to choose the 
single "winner" and will be triggered.
6. Group Selection Mode ( not on picture ): Use insertion order instead of weight, first come 
first served.
7. Key Match Priority: Prioritize by number of matched keys, the more keywords in the reply 
match, the higher chance it will be triggered.
=== PAGE 8 ===
How to make Lorebook/Script?
Here we will learn about making an entry of Lorebook/Script
Tips
1. Don’t need to add “ . “ in the keyword.
2. Don’t put the same keyword in the different entries of THE SAME CHARACTER
3. You can add multiple Lorebook/Script into the same character.
4. Lorebook/Script ≠ Advanced Script
5. Don’t use ALWAYS INCLUDE THIS ENTRY, you don’t need it in most case, your 
entry will always be added into the chat AS LONG AS the keywords and conditions 
were triggered
6. Keep each entry’s tokens at bay, you DON’T NEED to write a 10k tokens essay for a 
single entry.
7. Try to keep each entry’s token maximum 500 tokens, for my opinions, total tokens of 
Lorebook/Script can be large as long as you did each entry well and added a good 
keyword ( not overlap )
=== PAGE 9 ===
Now, it’s time for lesson ~
BASIC FOR DUMMIES
Why ALWAYS INCLUDE THIS ENTRY is HARMFUL?
First, it’s not necessary to always include this entry. When you click it, the entry will ALWAYS 
include in EVERY MESSAGE the user send to AI. It will cause:
1. Tokens wasted
2. The AI become delulu faster
3. Reply slower
4. More errors because of overload.
5. If the user pay per tokens, it’s even worse → pay more.
So, when you can use ALWAYS INCLUDE THIS ENTRY?
1. Overall summary setting of the world
2. NPC lists
And done, leave it, stay away from that button!!!!
Now, let’s sit down and start the lessons.
1. What is the most basic way to make an entry of lorebook?
The simplest and most basic way to make an entry of lorebook is “Send/write X then Y”, 
which mean when you send a keyword to the AI, the entry will be triggered.
Example: 
Lucy is Max’s girlfriend, she likes apple, banana, but she hates durian.
Keywords: Lucy, girlfriend, apple, durian.
{{user}}: Who is Lucy? ( Lucy is keyword → entry was triggered on bot’s reply )
{{char}}: Oh, she is Max’s girlfriend, don’t gift her durian, she hates it.
2. Ok, then what’s the use of Secondary Keywords?
See them as a lock for your entry.
Here I have a content: Lucy is Max’s girlfriend. She loves apple and banana, but she intensely 
hates durian. She finds the smell unbearable to her.
Main Keyword: Lucy
=== PAGE 10 ===
AND ANY
Secondary keys: apple, banana

→ Lucy is a main keyword but no secondary keys were included, entry won’t 
be triggered.
{{user}}: Who is Lucy, the girl eating an apple?
-> Lucy is a main keyword, “apple” is keyword of AND ANY and was 
included, entry will be triggered.
AND ALL
Secondary keys: Max, apple.
{{user}}: {{user}} sees Lucy is sitting next to Max and eating an apple.
→ Lucy is a main keyword, “Max” and “apple” are included, entry will be
triggered.
NOT ANY
Secondary keys: orange, grape, pear
{{user}}: “Oh, Lucy is buying orange juice with Max.”
→ Lucy is a main keyword, “orange” is key of NOT ALL ⇒ No entry will be
triggered
NOT ALL
Secondary keys: fruit, market
A. {{user}}: “Lucy is buying fruit in the market.”
→ Lucy is a main keyword, “fruit” and “market” are keys of NOT ALL ⇒
Entry won’t be triggered.
B. {{user}}: “Lucy is buying fruit with me.”
→ Lucy is a main keyword, “fruit” is keys of NOT ALL but “market” not 
included ⇒ Entry will be triggered.
3. What’s Probability?
This is the chance that the entry will be triggered.
Example: Lucy is Max’s girlfriend.
Main keyword: Lucy
=== PAGE 11 ===
Probability: 50% ( Default: 100% )
{{user}}: “Who is Lucy?”
→ Lucy is the keyword, but the Probability is 50, which mean you only have 50%
chance the entry will be triggered.
4. What is the meaning of Min Messages?
This will control after how many messages the entry will become active.
Example: Example: Lucy is Max’s girlfriend.
Main keyword: Lucy
Min messages: 10 ( Default: 0 )
{{user}}: “Who is Lucy?” ( first message from user )
→ Lucy is the keyword, but the Min messages is 10, which mean the entry won’t be
triggered
But IF:
{{user}}: “Who is Lucy?” ( 20th messages from user )
→ Lucy is the keyword, the Min messages is 10, now the message is the 20th ⇒ entry 
will be triggered

her table. ( keywords: Lucy, office, working )

places connect with Lucy

group (Lucy_Location), only one will be triggered.

2. What is Group Weight?
Think this: Inclusion Groups is a group of friends fighting and trying to take your attention, then 
Group Weight is the man control your crazy friends, his rule is simple: Let’s the luck decide 
everything.

=== PAGE 13 ===

keywords were matched.

keywords of A, B and C but C has 3 keywords: “Lucy”, “exercise”, “gym”
→ C will be triggered
=== PAGE 14 ===
WHAT A LOREBOOK/SCRIPT CAN DO?
Oh! It’s a lot

imagine? A basic Scenario can achieve that.

wife?

cons?

can add her/his full information in the lorebook, trigger with keywords, and it will 
add the depth into how NPC will act and talk in each situation ( No more cold dd 
boss become affection and kind in the other bots ).
Yeah, that is when Lorebook/Script steps into the play.
Besides, did you know you can use Lorebook to make a simple random event? ( still testing, 
some models work, some not. )

So, these are what lorebook can do (except what I said before):

4. Adding NPCs into the chat ( make a simple NPC list with few information )
5. Random events.
6. Keeps tokens count at bay for heavy lore’s bot.

{"returncode":0,"stdout":"=== PAGE 1 ===
Lorebook or Script in JanitorAI is a powerful tool to expand your world, characters and more 
than that.
Now, imagine you've made a wonderful, special cup of coffee and want to share the secret. 
Before sharing it with anyone, you must document the guide or recipe, that document is your 
Lorebook/Script.
So, what a Lorebook/Script can do?
1. Expand your world, specially if your book is the fiction one.
2. Expand your side characters, events, monsters or whatever like that.
3. Control and trigger memory that you want your bot to remember
4. Guides reaction about event, character, or something.
5. Trigger Event ( testing )
English not my first language so sometimes, there will be typo or sound like AI, please forgive 
me.
If you see any mistakes, feel free to inform me u/peipei1998 (reddit), I’ll fix it as soon as 
possible.
Source:
Help JanitorAI
Lorebook Basic Visual Guide
=== PAGE 2 ===
Where is Lorebook/Script?
1. Until now, only creators can add Lorebook/Script to the bots.
2. How to access to make Lorebook/Script?
Choose “Script beta” Choose “+”
Enter “Script name”, choose “Lorebook”
=== PAGE 3 ===
Click “Create Script” after you done.
MESSAGE DEPTH: How many messages will be available ( How many messages the script 
will add information after triggered.)
=== PAGE 4 ===
What inside Lorebook/Script?
Everything about making the lorebook will be in “How to make Lorebook/Script”
A. This is what you will see after click “Create script”.
1. Assign to your Characters : Click on it to choose the characters that you want to add the 
lorebook
2. After done with the Lorebook, choose the characters and press PUBLISH ( This 
button also use as Save ), and no, your lorebook/script is safe from being published, 
which only can be changed in CONFIGURE’s Visibility )
2.
B. Click “No keys” and you will see this:
1: On/off button of this Lorebook’s entry
=== PAGE 5 ===
2. ⚠ IMPORTANT: Do not check the 'Always include this entry' option unless you require 
this information to be visible to the bot in every single turn of the conversation. Lorebook is 
designed to save space: entries will always be added to the chat AS LONG AS their keywords 
or conditions are triggered. Overusing 'Always Include' will hurt your bot's memory for 
things that matter.
3+4+5: Only need to organize your entries.
C. Drag down.
1:
Where you put your keywords/trigger words for the entry. You MUST put at least one word in 
this field, even if the entry is set to be Always Active (Force Inclusion). If you leave it blank, 
you will receive an error when you try to save or publish the entry. ( Format for keyword: A, B, 
C, D…ect. 
🚫 Critical Rule: DO NOT use periods (.) or other punctuation within the keywords 
themselves, as this can break the triggering system. ( NO ONE want entry will be trigged 
everytime you send “ . “ )
2 + 3: There are already the example on the picture.
Case Sensitive: bless ≠ Bless ( name )
Match Whole Words: king ≠ liking ≠ Viking
=== PAGE 6 ===
D.
1+2. Secondary Keys : Mostly won’t need for a very basic Lorebook but can be useful for the 
complex one.
AND ANY The Entry will activate if at least one of these keywords is present in 
user’s reply.
AND ALL All keywords listed here must be present together in user’s reply
NOT ANY If any keyword in this list appears in the reply, the entry will be 
blocked from activating, even if the main keyword is present.
NOT ALL The entry will only be blocked if all keywords in this list appear 
together. If only one or some of the words appear, the entry can still 
be activated.
3. The content of Lorebook aka entry, where you put the information of side character, event, 
or something about the world setting…ect.
4. The same as Message Depth.
 
=== PAGE 7 ===
E.
1. Don’t touch it.
2. Min Messages: Active the script after how many messages.
3. Probability: The % the Entry will be triggered, default is 100%
4. Group Label: Label the entry.
Example: Everything about side character A can be labeled to the same label “Character A”. 
Only one entry from each group will be activated.
5. Group weight: Default is 100%, If more than one Entry in the same group is activated, the 
system uses the "Group Weight" values to perform a weighted random selection to choose the 
single "winner" and will be triggered.
6. Group Selection Mode ( not on picture ): Use insertion order instead of weight, first come 
first served.
7. Key Match Priority: Prioritize by number of matched keys, the more keywords in the reply 
match, the higher chance it will be triggered.
=== PAGE 8 ===
How to make Lorebook/Script?
Here we will learn about making an entry of Lorebook/Script
Tips
1. Don’t need to add “ . “ in the keyword.
2. Don’t put the same keyword in the different entries of THE SAME CHARACTER
3. You can add multiple Lorebook/Script into the same character.
4. Lorebook/Script ≠ Advanced Script
5. Don’t use ALWAYS INCLUDE THIS ENTRY, you don’t need it in most case, your 
entry will always be added into the chat AS LONG AS the keywords and conditions 
were triggered
6. Keep each entry’s tokens at bay, you DON’T NEED to write a 10k tokens essay for a 
single entry.
7. Try to keep each entry’s token maximum 500 tokens, for my opinions, total tokens of 
Lorebook/Script can be large as long as you did each entry well and added a good 
keyword ( not overlap )
=== PAGE 9 ===
Now, it’s time for lesson ~
BASIC FOR DUMMIES
Why ALWAYS INCLUDE THIS ENTRY is HARMFUL?
First, it’s not necessary to always include this entry. When you click it, the entry will ALWAYS 
include in EVERY MESSAGE the user send to AI. It will cause:
1. Tokens wasted
2. The AI become delulu faster
3. Reply slower
4. More errors because of overload.
5. If the user pay per tokens, it’s even worse → pay more.
So, when you can use ALWAYS INCLUDE THIS ENTRY?
1. Overall summary setting of the world
2. NPC lists
And done, leave it, stay away from that button!!!!
Now, let’s sit down and start the lessons.

The simplest and most basic way to make an entry of lorebook is “Send/write X then Y”,
