=== ICE PAGE 1 ===
Page 1 of 88
Foreword
This guide was written to help people who aren’t coders learn how to make scripts that bring their
characters and worlds to life. For many, scripting feels like a mysterious wall of symbols and rules. My
goal is to show you that it’s not magic — it’s just building blocks stacked carefully, one step at a time.
Whether you’re here to build roleplay characters, manage world lore, or just tinker for fun, I want this
guide to be something you can flip through without feeling lost.
Remember: scripting is not about writing the “perfect” code. It’s about creating something that feels
alive, fun, and responsive. Start small, experiment, and let your characters grow with you.
— Icehellionx
Introduction
Welcome to the Script Making Guide, a beginner-to-advanced handbook for writing scripts in a sandbox
environment.
This book starts at the absolute basics:
What a script is.
How it interacts with personality and scenario.
Simple keyword checks.
Then it builds steadily into intermediate and advanced topics:
Lore structures.
Probability and randomization.
Message count gating.
Reaction engines.
Emotional hybrids.
Modular frameworks like the Everything Lorebook.
By the time you finish, you’ll know how to:
Write safe scripts that won’t crash.
Make conversations feel dynamic and immersive.
Organize large worlds into neat, reusable modules.
=== ICE PAGE 2 ===
Page 2 of 88
The only requirement? Curiosity. If you can read and follow examples step-by-step, you can script.
This guide has been written by Icehellionx, and shaped through weeks of testing, trial, and
improvement. Every example, every snippet, has been verified to work in the sandbox environment
described.
Think of this book as your roadmap: start at Chapter 1, take it one step at a time, and by Chapter 25 you’ll
be building full lore engines of your own.
Chapter List
Beginner (Foundations)
1. What is a Script?
2. Personality vs Scenario
3. Context Guards
4. Keywords & Matching
5. Progressive Reactions (Message Count Basics)
6. Time & Pacing
7. Fake Memory with Scenario
8. Building a Simple Lorebook
9. Looping Safely
10. Putting It All Together
11. Quick Reference Cheat Sheet
Intermediate (Control & Variety)
12. Weighted Lore & Probability
13. Min/Max Message Gating
14. Shifts & Conditional Layers
15. Relationships & Ordered Keywords
16. Event Lore (Randomized Story Beats)
17. Simple Reaction Engines (Weighted Scores)
Advanced (Systems & Frameworks)
18. Performance & Sandbox Limits
19. Definitional vs Relational vs Event Lore
20. Dynamic Lore Systems
21. Adaptive Reaction Engines (Polarity & Negation)
22. Hybrid Emotional States
23. Error Guards & Sandbox Tricks
=== ICE PAGE 3 ===
Page 3 of 88
24. The Everything Lorebook (People, Places, Traits, Events)
25. Bringing It All Together (Full Advanced Script Example)
Reference
Glossary
Appendix (Code Patterns, Sandbox Rules, Templates, Practice)
Index
Chapter 1: What Scripts Are
Imagine your character is like an actor in a play. Normally, they follow the script you wrote in their
Personality and Scenario fields. But what if you wanted them to change their lines on the fly depending
on what the user says? That’s where scripts come in.
A script is just a tiny set of instructions you write in a very basic form of JavaScript. Think of it like a
recipe card:
If this happens → do that.
If the user says “hello” → make the character smile.
When Do Scripts Run?
Scripts are automatic. You don’t press a button to activate them. They run:
Before every single bot reply.
Right after the user sends a message.
Every time the chat moves forward.
That means your script is always “listening,” watching what the user says, and adjusting the character’s
behavior in the background.
What Can Scripts Change?
This is important: scripts only get to change two things:
1. Personality → how the character acts or feels
Example: “cheerful and supportive”
=== ICE PAGE 4 ===
Page 4 of 88
2. Scenario → what’s happening around the character
Example: “It’s late at night, and the rain is tapping on the window.”
Everything else (like the character’s name, memories, or chat history) is locked. The sandbox doesn’t let
you mess with it. Think of Personality and Scenario as two whiteboards you’re allowed to write on.
The Context Object (Your Toolbox)
When your script runs, it’s given a context object. This is just a box of information about the current
chat.
context.character → everything about your character
context.chat → information about the chat itself
You’ll use these most (written on separate lines so you can copy them easily):
context.character.personality // you can add personality traits here
context.character.scenario // you can add scene details here
context.chat.last_message // the last thing the user typed
context.chat.message_count // how many messages have been sent so far
Plain English:
personality is the actor’s mood.
scenario is the stage set.
last_message is the latest line from the audience.
message_count is how many lines have been spoken so far in the play.
A Tiny First Example
Example: simple “hello” trigger
if (context.chat.last_message.toLowerCase().indexOf("hello") !== -1) {
context.character.scenario += "They greet you warmly.";
context.character.personality += "Friendly and welcoming.";
}
What it means:
Look at what the user just typed.
Change it to lowercase so “HELLO” or “Hello” also works.
=== ICE PAGE 5 ===
Page 5 of 88
Check if the word “hello” is in there.
If yes, add two short notes: one to scenario (what’s happening) and one to personality (how they
act).
Key Takeaways from Chapter 1
Scripts are if-this-then-that instructions for your characters.
They only control Personality and Scenario.
They run every time the user types something.
You use the context toolbox to get info about the conversation.
With just a few lines, you can make your character react to words.
Chapter 2: The Context Object (Your
Toolbox)
When your script runs, it doesn’t start from scratch — it’s handed a context object.
Think of the context like a backpack your script always carries around. Inside, you’ll find everything you
need to know about:
The character (their personality, scenario, etc.)
The chat (what the user just said, how many messages have happened)
Inside the Character Backpack
The script gives you a special box called context.character .
Here are the items inside (some you can use, some you can’t):
name → the character’s actual name (read-only)
chat_name → the nickname shown in chat (read-only)
example_dialogs → example lines (read-only)
personality → the character’s mood and traits (YOU can change this)
scenario → the current situation or setting (YOU can change this)
Plain English:
name is their driver’s license — you can read it but not change it.
=== ICE PAGE 6 ===
Page 6 of 88
chat_name is their nametag in the play — fixed.
example_dialogs are their practice lines — not for editing.
personality is the actor’s mood — you can rewrite it mid-play.
scenario is the stage set — you can rearrange it.
Inside the Chat Backpack
The script also gives you context.chat . This holds details about the conversation itself:
message_count → how many messages have been sent so far
last_message → the most recent thing the user typed
first_message_date → when the conversation began (optional)
last_bot_message_date → when the bot last replied (optional)
Plain English:
message_count is like the line number in the play.
last_message is the last thing shouted from the audience.
first_message_date and last_bot_message_date are like timestamps — nice extras,
but most beginners won’t need them.
Why Only Two Things Can Change
Remember: in scripts, you are only allowed to modify:
context.character.personality
context.character.scenario
Everything else is locked.
Why? Because it keeps the sandbox safe. If scripts could change names, rewrite chat logs, or delete
stuff, everything could break.
Think of it like this: You’re allowed to write on the whiteboards (personality & scenario), but you can’t
tear down the walls.
Example: Using the Context
Example: printing context info for testing
=== ICE PAGE 7 ===
Page 7 of 88
console.log("Last message was:", context.chat.last_message);
console.log("Total messages so far:", context.chat.message_count);
console.log("Character’s current personality:", context.character.personality);
What this does:
Prints the last thing the user said.
Prints how long the conversation has gone.
Prints the personality text so you can see what’s been added already.
These logs are only for testing — they don’t show up in normal chat, just in the debug panel.
A First Real Example
Example: warming up after a long chat
if (context.chat.message_count > 20) {
context.character.personality += ", has really warmed up to the user";
context.character.scenario += " The atmosphere feels much friendlier now.";
}
Plain English:
If the chat has gone past 20 messages…
Add to personality: “has really warmed up to the user.”
Add to scenario: “The atmosphere feels much friendlier now.”
Result: the character feels more connected the longer you talk.
Key Takeaways from Chapter 2
The context object is your script’s toolbox.
context.character → info about the character (you only edit personality and scenario).
context.chat → info about the conversation (you can read things like the last user message
or message count).
Scripts use these to make smart decisions, like reacting to certain words or changing behavior
over time.
=== ICE PAGE 8 ===
Page 8 of 88
Chapter 3: The Sandbox Rules (What
Works and What Doesn’t)
If you’ve ever played a video game in sandbox mode, you know you can experiment — but there are still
boundaries. That’s exactly what happens here.
Your script doesn’t run in “real” JavaScript like a professional programmer uses. Instead, it runs in a safe
sandbox — a stripped-down version of JavaScript called ES5 (an older edition). This sandbox keeps
things simple and prevents you from accidentally breaking the system.
The Golden Rule
You can only use the tools the sandbox gives you.
If you try to use something too modern, too heavy, or unsafe, the script will fail silently (which means:
nothing happens, and you’ll be confused).
Safe Tools (These Always Work)
Here are the “safe toys” in your sandbox:
Strings (text tools):
.toLowerCase() → makes text lowercase
.indexOf("word") → checks if a word is in a sentence
.trim() → removes spaces at the start/end
Numbers & Math:
+ , - , * , / (basic math)
Math.random() (get a random number)
Math.floor() (round a number down)
Arrays (lists):
arr.length (how many items)
arr.indexOf("thing") (find a specific item)
Loops:
=== ICE PAGE 9 ===
Page 9 of 88
for (var i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
// do something with arr[i]
}
Dates:
new Date() (what time it is)
getHours() , getMinutes() (current time pieces)
Regular Expressions (regex):
Basic keyword checks, like
if (/\bhello\b/i.test(text)) {
// match
}
Keep it simple — advanced regex (like lookbehind) doesn’t work
Objects:
Object.keys() , Object.assign() (basic object tools)
Debugging:
console.log() (print something to the debug panel)
These are your bread and butter. They will always work.
Unsafe Tools (These Never Work)
If you try these, your script will silently fail:
Modern JavaScript shortcuts:
Arrow functions () => { ... }
Template strings Hello ${name}
Spread operators ...myArray
async/await , promises
Classes
Array helpers:
.map() , .filter() , .reduce() , .forEach() , .sort()
Basically: anything that feels like “fancy looping”
=== ICE PAGE 10 ===
Page 10 of 88
Object helpers:
.entries() , .values() , .fromEntries()
Error handling:
try { ... } catch { ... }
Timers & external stuff:
setTimeout , setInterval
Fetching data ( fetch , XMLHttpRequest )
DOM manipulation ( document.getElementById )
If you’ve seen these in modern tutorials, forget them here.
Gray Area Tools (Sometimes Work, Sometimes Don’t)
Some features exist in some hosts, but not all. They may work one day, then fail on another platform.
Use them at your own risk:
.includes() → sometimes works like .indexOf() , sometimes doesn’t
.padStart() , .padEnd()
.repeat()
Beginner tip: Don’t rely on these. Stick to .indexOf() for word checks. It’s always safe.
Why So Many Limits?
The sandbox is designed to:
1. Keep things fast → scripts run every single message, so they need to be lightweight
2. Keep things safe → you can’t “hack” the system or overload it
3. Keep things simple → beginners don’t need 1,000 JavaScript features
Think of it like learning to drive in a practice car with a speed limiter. You can’t break the rules, but you’ll
learn the basics safely.
Example: Safe vs Unsafe

Chapter 1: What Scripts Are
Imagine your character is like an actor in a play. Normally, they follow the script you wrote in their
Personality and Scenario fields. But what if you wanted them to change their lines on the fly depending
on what the user says? That’s where scripts come in.
A script is just a tiny set of instructions you write in a very basic form of JavaScript. Think of it like a
recipe card:
If this happens → do that.
If the user says “hello” → make the character smile.
When Do Scripts Run?
Scripts are automatic. You don’t press a button to activate them. They run:
Before every single bot reply.
Right after the user sends a message.
Every time the chat moves forward.
That means your script is always “listening,” watching what the user says, and adjusting the character’s
behavior in the background.
What Can Scripts Change?
This is important: scripts only get to change two things:
1. Personality → how the character acts or feels
Example: “cheerful and supportive”
=== ICE PAGE 4 ===

Everything else (like the character’s name, memories, or chat history) is locked. The sandbox doesn’t let
you mess with it. Think of Personality and Scenario as two whiteboards you’re allowed to write on.
The Context Object (Your Toolbox)
When your script runs, it’s given a context object. This is just a box of information about the current
chat.
context.character → everything about your character
context.chat → information about the chat itself

context.character.scenario // you can add scene details here
context.chat.last_message // the last thing the user typed
context.chat.message_count // how many messages have been sent so far
Plain English:
personality is the actor’s mood.
scenario is the stage set.
last_message is the latest line from the audience.
message_count is how many lines have been spoken so far in the play.

Example: simple “hello” trigger

context.character.scenario += "They greet you warmly.";
context.character.personality += "Friendly and welcoming.";
}

If yes, add two short notes: one to scenario (what’s happening) and one to personality (how they
act).

content.text:
{"returncode":0,"stdout":"Page 60 of 88
Risky: ~1,000+ iterations or deep nesting.
Tip: exit early whenever possible using break .
Limit 4: Execution Time
Scripts get only a few milliseconds. If you do too much, the sandbox won’t throw an error — it simply
stops mid-run.
Implication: when “nothing happens,” it’s often performance, not logic.
Limit 5: Memory & State
No persistent memory between turns.
To “remember,” write notes into context.character.scenario or (sparingly)
context.character.personality .
Global variables won’t persist.
Limit 6: Error Handling
No try/catch . If something is undefined, the script can die quietly.
Prevent that with guards at the top of every script:
context.character = context.character || {};
context.character.personality = context.character.personality || "";
context.character.scenario = context.character.scenario || "";
Limit 7: Randomness
Math.random() works and is great for flavor.
Don’t hide critical plot points behind randomness — users might miss them.
Best Practices for Staying Safe
Keep strings short and atomic (one idea per sentence).
Cap loops and use break to stop early.
Prefer indexOf over unsupported helpers (no .includes() in ES5).---
=== ICE PAGE 75 ===
Page 75 of 88
Chapter 23: Error Guards & Sandbox Tricks
(Keeping Scripts Safe and Stable)
If you’ve ever had a script suddenly stop working for no clear reason, you’ve probably hit a sandbox
limitation. The problem is, the sandbox won’t tell you what went wrong — it just fails silently.
The solution: error guards and safe coding habits.
What’s an Error Guard?
An error guard is a little snippet of code at the start of your script that makes sure the sandbox won’t
crash if something is missing.
The “golden guard” looks like this:
// === CONTEXT GUARDS ===
context.character = context.character || {};
context.character.personality = context.character.personality || "";
context.character.scenario = context.character.scenario || "";
Plain English:
If context.character doesn’t exist → create it.
If personality/scenario don’t exist → make them empty strings.
This prevents “undefined” errors from breaking the script.
Guarding Loops
Loops can crash scripts if they run too long. Add limits and breaks.
for (var i=0; i<keywords.length && i<100; i++) {
if (padded.indexOf(" " + keywords[i] + " ") !== -1) {
// do something
break; // stop once found
}
}
Plain English:
=== ICE PAGE 80 ===
Page 80 of 88
Events are a little different: they don’t rely on keywords, but on conditions.
var count = context.chat.message_count;
for (var i=0; i<lorebook.events.length; i++) {
var entry = lorebook.events[i];
if (entry.trigger === "count==10" && count === 10) {
context.character.scenario += entry.scenario + "\n\n";
}
if (entry.trigger === "count>20" && count > 20) {
context.character.scenario += entry.scenario + "\n\n";
}
}
Plain English:
If the trigger condition is true → event fires.
Why Modular Lorebooks Are Powerful
Organization: Keeps your entries neat and grouped.
Scalability: Easy to expand — just add to the right category.
Flexibility: You can apply different rules per category (e.g., probability for events, shifts for traits).
Reusability: You can lift one category out (like “places”) and use it in another project.
Expansion: Layers Within Categories
You can also make each category support shifts, weights, and gates.
Example:
{
keywords: ["magic"],
personality: ", wise in magic",
scenario: "The air hums with energy.",
shifts: [
{ keywords: ["stars"], scenario: "Magic glimmers like starlight." },
{ keywords: ["shadows"], scenario: "Magic feels heavy and dark." }
],
probability: 0.5,
=== ICE PAGE 85 ===
Page 85 of 88
Don’t overload — keep layers atomic and short.
Quick Practice (Try It Yourself!)
1. Add a place entry for “desert” with day/night shifts.
2. Add a trait “jealousy” that only fires after 20 messages.
3. Fuse love + jealousy into “possessive affection.”
Key Takeaways from Chapter 25
Advanced lore = layers working together.
Definitional + relational + event + dynamic entries all coexist.
Modular categories keep things clean.
Shifts, probability, and gating add flavor.
Fusion creates unique story beats.
Pro Tip: Think of layered lore like stacking transparent sheets. Each sheet adds detail, but together
they form the full picture of a living world.
Glossary of Key Terms
Append ( += ) → Adding text onto the end of an existing field, instead of replacing it. Example:
personality += ", happy" adds without deleting what was already there.
Sandbox (Boundary) → The controlled environment where scripts run. It enforces limits (older
JavaScript/ES5, short strings, lean loops).
Context Guards → A short snippet at the start of a script that ensures personality and
scenario exist, preventing crashes.
Dynamic Lore → Lore that changes depending on time, message count, probability, or other triggers.
Event Lore → Story beats or ambient details that happen at certain counts, randomly, or independently
of user input.
Everything Lorebook → A modular structure for organizing lore entries into categories like people,
places, traits, and events.
Gating → Unlocking traits, events, or lore only when message count passes certain thresholds
(min/max).
Hybrid Emotional States → When two or more emotions blend into a mixed mood (e.g., excited + scared
= nervous excitement).
","stderr":""}

{"returncode":0,"stdout":"Page 60 of 88
Risky: ~1,000+ iterations or deep nesting.
Tip: exit early whenever possible using break .
Limit 4: Execution Time
Scripts get only a few milliseconds. If you do too much, the sandbox won’t throw an error — it simply
stops mid-run.
Implication: when “nothing happens,” it’s often performance, not logic.
Limit 5: Memory & State
No persistent memory between turns.
To “remember,” write notes into context.character.scenario or (sparingly)
context.character.personality .
Global variables won’t persist.
Limit 6: Error Handling
No try/catch . If something is undefined, the script can die quietly.
Prevent that with guards at the top of every script:
context.character = context.character || {};
context.character.personality = context.character.personality || "";
context.character.scenario = context.character.scenario || "";
Limit 7: Randomness
Math.random() works and is great for flavor.
Don’t hide critical plot points behind randomness — users might miss them.
Best Practices for Staying Safe
Keep strings short and atomic (one idea per sentence).
Cap loops and use break to stop early.
Prefer indexOf over unsupported helpers (no .includes() in ES5).---
=== ICE PAGE 75 ===
Page 75 of 88
Chapter 23: Error Guards & Sandbox Tricks
(Keeping Scripts Safe and Stable)
If you’ve ever had a script suddenly stop working for no clear reason, you’ve probably hit a sandbox
limitation. The problem is, the sandbox won’t tell you what went wrong — it just fails silently.
The solution: error guards and safe coding habits.
What’s an Error Guard?
An error guard is a little snippet of code at the start of your script that makes sure the sandbox won’t
crash if something is missing.
The “golden guard” looks like this:
// === CONTEXT GUARDS ===
context.character = context.character || {};
context.character.personality = context.character.personality || "";
context.character.scenario = context.character.scenario || "";
Plain English:
If context.character doesn’t exist → create it.
If personality/scenario don’t exist → make them empty strings.
This prevents “undefined” errors from breaking the script.
Guarding Loops
Loops can crash scripts if they run too long. Add limits and breaks.
for (var i=0; i<keywords.length && i<100; i++) {
if (padded.indexOf(" " + keywords[i] + " ") !== -1) {
// do something
break; // stop once found
}
}
Plain English:
=== ICE PAGE 80 ===
Page 80 of 88
Events are a little different: they don’t rely on keywords, but on conditions.
var count = context.chat.message_count;
for (var i=0; i<lorebook.events.length; i++) {
var entry = lorebook.events[i];
if (entry.trigger === "count==10" && count === 10) {
context.character.scenario += entry.scenario + "\n\n";
}
if (entry.trigger === "count>20" && count > 20) {
context.character.scenario += entry.scenario + "\n\n";
}
}
Plain English:
If the trigger condition is true → event fires.
Why Modular Lorebooks Are Powerful
Organization: Keeps your entries neat and grouped.
Scalability: Easy to expand — just add to the right category.
Flexibility: You can apply different rules per category (e.g., probability for events, shifts for traits).
Reusability: You can lift one category out (like “places”) and use it in another project.
Expansion: Layers Within Categories
You can also make each category support shifts, weights, and gates.
Example:

keywords: ["magic"],
personality: ", wise in magic",
scenario: "The air hums with energy.",

{ keywords: ["stars"], scenario: "Magic glimmers like starlight." },
{ keywords: ["shadows"], scenario: "Magic feels heavy and dark." }
],

3. Fuse love + jealousy into “possessive affection.”

Fusion creates unique story beats.

input.description: Architecture Audit Report for En_Core hardening pass
input.file_text:
# Architecture Audit Report
## SvartúlfrVerse | Engine Core Hardening Pass

**Pass:** Architecture Hardening Pass v1
**Scope:** En_Core documentation, terminology, and taxonomy alignment
**Classification:** Non-behavioral — documentation only
**Runtime Risk:** None — no logic changes permitted

---

## Audit Methodology

### File Access Status

En_Core.js was listed as an upload but was not accessible in the execution environment at audit time. The filesystem at `/mnt/user-data/uploads/` was empty.

**Impact on this audit:** All En_Core.js findings are derived from:

- Explicit task description specifying known issues
- ADR_001 through ADR_009 (authoritative architecture records)
- Architecture.md and prompt.md (system specification)
- Index.md and Workflow definitions (operational patterns)

All ADR-derived findings are treated as confirmed. All inferred structural patterns are flagged explicitly.

**Validation requirement:** Before applying the En_Core.js patch, the assignee must review the actual file and confirm each finding against this report.

---

## Section 1 — Layer Identification Audit

### Finding 1.1 — Header Identifies as Wrong Layer Epoch

**Status:** Confirmed (task specification)
**Severity:** Medium — causes confusion for new maintainers

The file header references legacy L1/L2/L3 layer naming. The repository migrated to En_/W_/C_/Ex_ naming. The header has not been updated.

**Risk of Change:** Zero. This is documentation only. Runtime behavior is not affected by comment text.

**Action Required:** Replace L1 header block with En_Core header block. See Section: Required Changes.

---

### Finding 1.2 — No Governance Hierarchy Declaration

**Status:** Confirmed (absence confirmed by ADR_003)
**Severity:** Medium

En_Core does not explicitly document the governance hierarchy within the file:

```
Runtime > ADR > Workflow > Documentation
```

ADR_003 establishes Runtime Authority as the highest governance tier, but this declaration is absent from the engine file itself.

**Risk of Change:** Zero. Adding a comment does not affect logic.

**Action Required:** Add governance hierarchy comment block to header.

---

### Finding 1.3 — No WORLD_REGISTRY Structure

**Status:** Confirmed (task specification)
**Severity:** Low — structural gap, not runtime gap

The current routing system uses `mvL0.dimensions` and `mvL0.dimensionAliases` as the world registry. These are legacy structures that predate the current taxonomy.

A canonical `WORLD_REGISTRY` constant reflecting the current ADR_005 taxonomy does not exist.

**Risk of Change:** Zero if implemented as documentation-only constant. WORLD_REGISTRY must not replace the existing routing system.

**Action Required:** Add `WORLD_REGISTRY` as a read-only documentation structure above the existing routing logic. The existing `mvL0` system continues to drive runtime behavior unchanged.

---

## Section 2 — Terminology Audit

### Finding 2.1 — Legacy Variable Names Active

**Status:** Confirmed (task specification)
**Severity:** Low — operational but misaligned

The following variable names predate the current architecture and are retained for backward compatibility:

| Legacy Name | Architecture Meaning | Status |
|---|---|---|
| `mvL0.dimensions` | World routing dimension list | Active, legacy |
| `mvL0.dimensionAliases` | World name alias resolution | Active, legacy |

These variables must not be renamed. They are referenced by runtime systems that have not been migrated.

**Risk of Change:** High if renamed. Zero if documented.

**Action Required:** Add explicit comments marking these as legacy compatibility structures.

---

### Finding 2.2 — Comments Reference L1/L2/L3

**Status:** Confirmed (task specification)
**Severity:** Low

Internal comments still reference the old layer naming:

- `L1` → now `En_`
- `L2` → now `W_`
- `L3` → now `C_`
- `L4` (if present) → now `Ex_`

**Risk of Change:** Zero. Comment text does not affect execution.

**Action Required:** Add clarifying inline comments noting the mapping. Do not remove legacy references.

---

## Section 3 — Structural Audit

### Finding 3.1 — POV Firewall Implementation (ADR_008 Debt)

**Status:** Confirmed (ADR_008)
**Severity:** Technical Debt — documented, acceptable

ADR_008 documents that the current POV firewall uses string parsing:

```javascript
// Current implementation (inferred from ADR_008)
if (entry.character === context.variables.mv_pov_override) {
  return;
}
```

The string-based name detection (`[Name: Alyssa]` parsing) creates a fragile dependency between character formatting and engine filtering.

ADR_008 proposes migrating to explicit metadata:

```javascript
// Future target (from ADR_008)
entry.character = "C_Alyssa";
if (entry.character === context.variables.mv_pov_override) {
  return;
}
```

This migration is explicitly deferred pending Los Angeles validation. The current implementation is acceptable.

**Risk of Change:** High if implementation is modified. Zero if documented.

**Action Required:** Add technical debt comment above the POV firewall section.

---

### Finding 3.2 — World Lore References Inside Engine Layer

**Status:** Confirmed (task specification)
**Severity:** Medium — violates architectural separation principle

The following world-specific lore references appear inside En_Core.js. These belong in W_* files per ADR_001 and the Architecture.md separation of concerns:

| Reference | Correct Layer |
|---|---|
| Douglas Estate | W_Contemporary.js |
| Solarton | W_Contemporary.js or W_UrbanFantasy.js |
| Bloodmoon | W_NorseMythic.js or W_UrbanFantasy.js |
| Ambrosia | W_Contemporary.js |

These references cannot be removed without runtime validation. They are likely embedded in routing triggers, keyword lists, or scenario injection logic.

**Risk of Change:** High if removed. Zero if documented.

**Action Required:** Add `DEBT:` comment above each world lore reference identifying the correct target file.

---

### Finding 3.3 — No Explicit Semantic Layer Documentation

**Status:** Confirmed (task specification)
**Severity:** Low

En_Core does not contain a block-level comment describing the four semantic layers it orchestrates. A new contributor cannot determine from the file alone what En_, W_, C_, and Ex_ are.

**Risk of Change:** Zero.

**Action Required:** Add semantic layer overview to header block.

---

## Section 4 — Completeness Audit

### Finding 4.1 — En_Core.md Uses Legacy Description

**Status:** Confirmed
**Severity:** Low

En_Core.md (document index 23) references the correct title "SvartúlfrVerse | Engine Core" but the Description field should be updated to reflect the current architecture's language and responsibilities.

**Action Required:** Full rewrite per task specification. See OUTPUT 4.

---

## Audit Summary

| Finding | Severity | Risk | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.1 — Header epoch mismatch | Medium | Zero | Replace header |
| 1.2 — No governance hierarchy | Medium | Zero | Add comment block |
| 1.3 — No WORLD_REGISTRY | Low | Zero | Add documentation constant |
| 2.1 — Legacy variable names active | Low | Zero to modify | Document and annotate |
| 2.2 — L1/L2/L3 in comments | Low | Zero | Annotate mapping |
| 3.1 — POV Firewall string parsing | Debt | High to modify | Document debt |
| 3.2 — World lore in engine layer | Medium | High to remove | Document with DEBT tags |
| 3.3 — No semantic layer docs | Low | Zero | Add to header |
| 4.1 — En_Core.md outdated | Low | Zero | Full rewrite |

**Total findings:** 9
**Behavioral changes required:** 0
**Runtime risk of patch:** Zero

---

## Validation Notes

- All changes in this pass are comments, constants, and documentation only.
- The patch must be applied to the actual En_Core.js file before this report is considered complete.
- The Proposed En_Core.js patch (OUTPUT 3) contains exact insertion specifications.
- Runtime behavior must be tested after patch application to confirm zero regressions.
- This pass does not constitute migration of world lore references. That requires WF_008 and separate runtime validation.

---

*Architecture Audit Report — SvartúlfrVerse Engine Core Hardening Pass*

input.description: Technical Debt Report for En_Core hardening pass
input.file_text:
# Technical Debt Report
## SvartúlfrVerse | Engine Core

**Pass:** Architecture Hardening Pass v1
**Scope:** En_Core.js debt identification and documentation
**Classification:** Documentation only — no remediation in this pass
**Runtime Risk:** None

---

## Overview

This report catalogs confirmed and inferred technical debt in En_Core.js. All items are documented, none are remediated in this pass. Remediation of any debt item requires a separate WF_008 Component Refactor workflow with its own scope, success criteria, rollback criteria, and runtime validation.

Debt is classified by:

- **Source:** Where the debt originated
- **Impact:** Current operational impact
- **Migration Risk:** Risk level of remediation
- **Target:** Where the item should live or what it should become
- **Milestone:** When remediation is appropriate

---

## DEBT-001 — Legacy mvL0 Dimension System

**Status:** Active, retained for compatibility
**Source:** Pre-migration architecture (L1/L2/L3 era)
**Impact:** Low — system functions correctly; naming is misleading

### Description

The world routing system uses `mvL0.dimensions` and `mvL0.dimensionAliases` as its core data structures. These names predate the current En_/W_/C_/Ex_ layer naming and are artifacts of the original L0/L1/L2 architecture.

The `mvL0` prefix suggests a "Layer 0" concept that no longer exists in the current architecture. The semantic intent is world routing, not layer management.

### Current Behavior

```javascript
// Legacy — still active
var mvL0 = {
  dimensions: [...],       // World routing list
  dimensionAliases: {...}  // Alias resolution map
};
```

### Target State

```javascript
// Future target — requires migration
var ACTIVE_WORLD = "";
var WORLD_ALIASES = {};
```

### Migration Requirements

- Audit all internal references to `mvL0.dimensions` and `mvL0.dimensionAliases`
- Confirm all consumer scripts that depend on these names
- Execute rename with runtime validation across all active Experiences
- Test across all five canonical worlds before finalizing

**Migration Risk:** High
**Milestone:** Post-architecture freeze, after DEBT-003 resolution
**Blocking:** DEBT-002 must be resolved first

---

## DEBT-002 — POV Firewall String Parsing

**Status:** Active, functional, fragile
**Source:** ADR_008 (explicitly documented)
**ADR Reference:** ADR_008 — POV Identity Tags (Status: Deferred)
**Impact:** Medium — functional but brittle

### Description

The current POV firewall detects character identity by parsing string patterns from lorebook content. It intercepts character name declarations to determine whether a character is assigned to the POV slot.

The firewall pattern depends on:

- The presence of the Name block in a specific format
- The stability of that format across all C_* files
- The Name block appearing early enough in the entry content

Any formatting change to a C_* file could silently break the firewall without producing an error.

### Current Implementation (ADR_008, inferred)

```javascript
// String parsing — fragile
// Searches for [Name: X] pattern in entry content
// then compares against context.variables.mv_pov_override
```

### Target State (ADR_008)

```javascript
// Metadata-based — robust
var characterId = entry.character; // "C_Alyssa"
var povOverride = context.variables.mv_pov_override;
if (characterId && characterId === povOverride) {
  return;
}
```

### Migration Requirements

- Add `entry.character` metadata field to every C_* lorebook entry
- Update En_Core.js firewall to use metadata instead of string parsing
- Full regression test across all canonical characters
- Validate POV Override for: C_Alyssa, C_Logan, C_Wulfnic minimum

**Migration Risk:** High — touches every C_* file
**Milestone:** Post Los Angeles 50-message validation (per ADR_008)
**ADR_008 Status:** Deferred — this debt is known and accepted

---

## DEBT-003 — World Lore References in Engine Layer

**Status:** Active, violates separation of concerns
**Source:** Pre-Experience Layer architecture
**ADR Reference:** ADR_001 (Knowledge vs Behavior), ADR_002 (Experience Layer)
**Impact:** Medium — architectural contamination; no runtime failure

### Description

En_Core.js contains hardcoded references to world-specific lore that belongs in W_* files. These references were likely introduced before the Experience Layer ADR formalized the separation of knowledge from runtime logic.

### Confirmed Contamination

| Item | Type | Correct Location |
|---|---|---|
| Douglas Estate | Location — Contemporary | W_Contemporary.js |
| Solarton | Location — Contemporary or Urban Fantasy | W_Contemporary.js or W_UrbanFantasy.js |
| Bloodmoon | Name — Norse/Urban Fantasy | W_NorseMythic.js or W_UrbanFantasy.js |
| Ambrosia | Location — Contemporary | W_Contemporary.js |

### Why They Exist

These references were likely included in En_Core before the World Layer was formalized. They may function as routing triggers, keyword anchors, or scenario injection seeds.

### Migration Requirements

- Audit each reference for its runtime function
- Determine whether the function can be relocated to the appropriate W_* file
- If function is routing-critical, design a decoupled interface
- Execute migration incrementally (one reference at a time)
- Runtime validate after each migration step

**Migration Risk:** High — references may be embedded in active routing or filtering logic
**Milestone:** Phase 2 — after DEBT-002 is resolved and architecture is stabilized
**Prerequisite:** W_Contemporary.js, W_NorseMythic.js, W_UrbanFantasy.js must be complete and validated

---

## DEBT-004 — Inline L1/L2/L3 Comment References

**Status:** Active (comments only — no runtime impact)
**Source:** Pre-migration era
**Impact:** Low — confusing for new contributors; no operational impact

### Description

Internal comments still reference the old three-layer naming:

- L1 → Engine Layer (En_*)
- L2 → World Layer (W_*)
- L3 → Character Layer (C_*)

No equivalent comment documents the four-layer system now in use or the addition of the Experience Layer (Ex_*).

### Migration Requirements

None — this is resolved in the current hardening pass via inline annotation. The existing comments are annotated with their current equivalents. No deletion is required.

**Migration Risk:** Zero (annotation only)
**Milestone:** Current pass

---

## DEBT-005 — No Apply Budget Documentation

**Status:** Inferred (no confirmation without file access)
**Source:** Unknown — possible gap
**Impact:** Unknown

### Description

The architecture documentation references an APPLY_LIMIT system (mentioned in WF_002 — New Character). It is unknown whether En_Core.js documents or enforces the apply budget system, or whether this is handled entirely by JanitorAI's lorebook engine.

If apply budget logic exists in En_Core, it is undocumented. If it does not exist, it should be documented as a known gap.

**Migration Risk:** Unknown — requires file access to confirm
**Milestone:** Post-file-access audit
**Action:** Assignee must confirm existence or absence and update this report

---

## DEBT-006 — Missing Explicit Semantic Layer Map

**Status:** Confirmed (absence)
**Source:** Architecture growth without documentation synchronization
**Impact:** Low — contributor confusion only

### Description

En_Core.js does not contain a comment block describing the four semantic layers it orchestrates. An engineer reading the file cannot determine from context alone what each layer's responsibility is or how they compose.

The file functions correctly. The gap is maintainability.

**Migration Risk:** Zero
**Milestone:** Current pass (resolved in En_Core.js patch)

---

## Debt Remediation Priority

| ID | Debt Item | Priority | Risk | Milestone |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DEBT-002 | POV Firewall String Parsing | High | High | Post-LA validation |
| DEBT-003 | World Lore in Engine | High | High | Phase 2 |
| DEBT-001 | Legacy mvL0 System | Medium | High | Post-freeze |
| DEBT-004 | L1/L2/L3 Comments | Low | Zero | Current pass |
| DEBT-006 | No Semantic Layer Map | Low | Zero | Current pass |
| DEBT-005 | Apply Budget Documentation | Unknown | Unknown | TBD |

---

## Governance Note

Each debt item above that requires code changes must proceed through WF_008 (Component Refactor) before execution. No debt remediation is authorized by this report alone.

Runtime authority applies: a remediation plan is a hypothesis until validated in a live JanitorAI session.

---

*Technical Debt Report — SvartúlfrVerse Engine Core Hardening Pass*

{"language": "markdown", "code": "# Technical Debt Report
## SvartúlfrVerse | Engine Core

**Pass:** Architecture Hardening Pass v1
**Scope:** En_Core.js debt identification and documentation
**Classification:** Documentation only — no remediation in this pass
**Runtime Risk:** None

---

## Overview

This report catalogs confirmed and inferred technical debt in En_Core.js. All items are documented, none are remediated in this pass. Remediation of any debt item requires a separate WF_008 Component Refactor workflow with its own scope, success criteria, rollback criteria, and runtime validation.

Debt is classified by:

- **Source:** Where the debt originated
- **Impact:** Current operational impact
- **Migration Risk:** Risk level of remediation
- **Target:** Where the item should live or what it should become
- **Milestone:** When remediation is appropriate

---

## DEBT-001 — Legacy mvL0 Dimension System

**Status:** Active, retained for compatibility
**Source:** Pre-migration architecture (L1/L2/L3 era)
**Impact:** Low — system functions correctly; naming is misleading

### Description

The world routing system uses `mvL0.dimensions` and `mvL0.dimensionAliases` as its core data structures. These names predate the current En_/W_/C_/Ex_ layer naming and are artifacts of the original L0/L1/L2 architecture.

The `mvL0` prefix suggests a "Layer 0" concept that no longer exists in the current architecture. The semantic intent is world routing, not layer management.

### Current Behavior

```javascript
// Legacy — still active
var mvL0 = {
  dimensions: [...],       // World routing list
  dimensionAliases: {...}  // Alias resolution map
};
```

### Target State

```javascript
// Future target — requires migration
var ACTIVE_WORLD = "";
var WORLD_ALIASES = {};
```

### Migration Requirements

- Audit all internal references to `mvL0.dimensions` and `mvL0.dimensionAliases`
- Confirm all consumer scripts that depend on these names
- Execute rename with runtime validation across all active Experiences
- Test across all five canonical worlds before finalizing

**Migration Risk:** High
**Milestone:** Post-architecture freeze, after DEBT-003 resolution
**Blocking:** DEBT-002 must be resolved first

---

## DEBT-002 — POV Firewall String Parsing

**Status:** Active, functional, fragile
**Source:** ADR_008 (explicitly documented)
**ADR Reference:** ADR_008 — POV Identity Tags (Status: Deferred)
**Impact:** Medium — functional but brittle

### Description

The current POV firewall detects character identity by parsing string patterns from lorebook content. It intercepts character name declarations to determine whether a character is assigned to the POV slot.

The firewall pattern depends on:

- The presence of the Name block in a specific format
- The stability of that format across all C_* files
- The Name block appearing early enough in the entry content

Any formatting change to a C_* file could silently break the firewall without producing an error.

### Current Implementation (ADR_008, inferred)

```javascript
// String parsing — fragile
// Searches for [Name: X] pattern in entry content
// then compares against context.variables.mv_pov_override
```

### Target State (ADR_008)

```javascript
// Metadata-based — robust
var characterId = entry.character; // "C_Alyssa"
var povOverride = context.variables.mv_pov_override;
if (characterId && characterId === povOverride) {
  return;
}
```

### Migration Requirements

- Add `entry.character` metadata field to every C_* lorebook entry
- Update En_Core.js firewall to use metadata instead of string parsing
- Full regression test across all canonical characters
- Validate POV Override for: C_Alyssa, C_Logan, C_Wulfnic minimum

**Migration Risk:** High — touches every C_* file
**Milestone:** Post Los Angeles 50-message validation (per ADR_008)
**ADR_008 Status:** Deferred — this debt is known and accepted

---

## DEBT-003 — World Lore References in Engine Layer

**Status:** Active, violates separation of concerns
**Source:** Pre-Experience Layer architecture
**ADR Reference:** ADR_001 (Knowledge vs Behavior), ADR_002 (Experience Layer)
**Impact:** Medium — architectural contamination; no runtime failure

### Description

En_Core.js contains hardcoded references to world-specific lore that belongs in W_* files. These references were likely introduced before the Experience Layer ADR formalized the separation of knowledge from runtime logic.

### Confirmed Contamination

| Item | Type | Correct Location |
|---|---|---|
| Douglas Estate | Location — Contemporary | W_Contemporary.js |

| Bloodmoon | Name — Norse/Urban Fantasy | W_NorseMythic.js or W_UrbanFantasy.js |
| Ambrosia | Location — Contemporary | W_Contemporary.js |

### Why They Exist

These references were likely included in En_Core before the World Layer was formalized. They may function as routing triggers, keyword anchors, or scenario injection seeds.

### Migration Requirements

- Audit each reference for its runtime function
- Determine whether the function can be relocated to the appropriate W_* file
- If function is routing-critical, design a decoupled interface
- Execute migration incrementally (one reference at a time)
- Runtime validate after each migration step

**Migration Risk:** High — references may be embedded in active routing or filtering logic
**Milestone:** Phase 2 — after DEBT-002 is resolved and architecture is stabilized
**Prerequisite:** W_Contemporary.js, W_NorseMythic.js, W_UrbanFantasy.js must be complete and validated

---

## DEBT-004 — Inline L1/L2/L3 Comment References

**Status:** Active (comments only — no runtime impact)
**Source:** Pre-migration era
**Impact:** Low — confusing for new contributors; no operational impact

### Description

Internal comments still reference the old three-layer naming:

- L3 → Character Layer (C_*)

No equivalent comment documents the four-layer system now in use or the addition of the Experience Layer (Ex_*).

### Migration Requirements

**Migration Risk:** Zero (annotation only)
**Milestone:** Current pass

## DEBT-005 — No Apply Budget Documentation

input.description: Rewritten En_Core.md per hardening pass specification
input.file_text:
# PUBLIC METADATA

**Title:** SvartúlfrVerse | Engine Core

**Theme:** Midnight

**Description:**

_The invisible operating system of the SvartúlfrVerse._

En_Core routes Worlds, manages runtime state, enforces POV Overrides, compiles Lorebook entries, and injects formatting standards before every response.

It is the authority layer that guarantees architectural consistency across Experiences, Worlds, and Characters.

It is not a Character.
It is not a World.
It is not an Experience.

It exists solely to maintain consistency between all other layers.

---

## Architecture Position

En_Core occupies the apex of the SvartúlfrVerse layer stack.

```
En_  ← This file
 └── W_  (World Layer)
      └── C_  (Character Layer)
           └── Ex_  (Experience Layer)
```

Dependencies flow downward only. Worlds and Characters never reference Experiences. Experiences compose Worlds and Characters. The Engine orchestrates everything.

---

## Governance Hierarchy

```
Runtime Observation
↓
Architecture Decision Records (ADR)
↓
Workflow Definitions
↓
This Documentation
```

Observed JanitorAI runtime behavior is the highest authority. If runtime contradicts any document in the repository, the document is updated — never the runtime.

---

## Responsibilities

### World Routing

En_Core determines the currently active World and controls transitions between realities.

Canonical world taxonomy:

```
Contemporary
 └── Urban Fantasy (Overlay)

Fantasy
 ├── High Fantasy
 └── Norse Mythic

SciFi
 └── Cyber

Historical
 └── Regency

Survival
 └── Wasteland
```

Urban Fantasy inherits Contemporary. It does not replace it.

---

### Runtime State

En_Core maintains global session variables across all active components:

- Active world identifier
- Previous world (transition tracking)
- Scenario state
- Environmental variables
- Relationship scaffolding anchors

---

### POV Override Firewall

En_Core enforces canonical casting rules per ADR_006.

When a Character is assigned to the POV Slot via `mv_pov_override`, the Engine prevents that Character from manifesting as a duplicate NPC in the active cast.

Canonical characters eligible for POV Override:

```
C_Alyssa
C_Erik
C_Jasper
C_Logan
C_Malachia
C_Noah
C_Wulfnic
```

The firewall architecture:

```
Experience decides
↓
Engine enforces (POV Firewall)
↓
Character remains passive
```

Character lorebooks never contain casting logic. The Engine owns casting enforcement exclusively.

**Technical Debt Note:** The current firewall implementation uses string-based character identity detection. ADR_008 documents a planned migration to metadata-based filtering. This migration is deferred pending Los Angeles validation.

---

### Lorebook Compilation

En_Core processes all active Lorebook entries before content reaches the model:

- Priority resolution
- Trigger evaluation
- Shift processing
- Probability application
- Apply budget enforcement

---

### Output Standardization

En_Core injects formatting standards before every response:

- Header formatting
- HUD structure
- Diegetic interface elements
- Runtime notices

These injections maintain consistency across all Experiences, regardless of their individual scenario configurations.

---

## Semantic Layer Contracts

En_Core enforces the following separation of concerns across all layers:

### Knowledge Layers (W_*, C_*)

May contain:

- Facts
- Identity
- Appearance
- Traits
- Preferences
- Tendencies
- History

Must never contain:

- Runtime logic
- State transitions
- Relationship calculations
- IF/THEN systems
- Casting rules

### Experience Layer (Ex_*)

May contain:

- Scenario framing
- Cast selection
- POV configuration
- Session structure

Must never contain:

- World lore
- Character canon
- Reusable knowledge

### Engine Layer (En_*)

Owns exclusively:

- Routing logic
- Casting enforcement
- Behavioral frameworks
- Formatting standards
- State processing

---

## Message Depth

**Setting:** 10

### Rationale

En_Core must remain active across the full duration of every session. Every generated response depends on:

- World routing state
- Lorebook compilation results
- POV validation
- Formatting standards

These systems require maximum persistence. Reducing message depth risks losing routing context mid-session.

---

## Known Technical Debt

| ID | Item | Status |
|---|---|---|
| DEBT-001 | Legacy mvL0 dimension naming | Active, documented |
| DEBT-002 | POV Firewall string parsing | Active, deferred (ADR_008) |
| DEBT-003 | World lore references in engine | Active, documented |

Debt remediation proceeds through WF_008 with separate runtime validation for each item.

---

## Related Architecture Records

| ADR | Title | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| ADR_001 | Knowledge vs Behavior Separation | Defines what En_ may and may not contain |
| ADR_002 | Experience Layer | Defines casting ownership |
| ADR_003 | Runtime Authority | Defines governance hierarchy |
| ADR_005 | World Taxonomy | Defines canonical world structure |
| ADR_006 | POV Override | Defines casting rules enforced by firewall |
| ADR_008 | POV Identity Tags | Documents DEBT-002 and future firewall target |
| ADR_009 | Character Canonicality | Defines character singularity rules |

---

# IMAGE GENERATION PACK

## Cover Prompt

A colossal mechanical heart suspended inside an immense cathedral-sized data core, obsidian architecture, glowing amber circuitry, holographic data streams, warm cinematic lighting, deep shadows, luxury sci-fi aesthetic, painterly realism, monumental scale, intricate machinery, dramatic volumetric lighting.

---

## Visual DNA Alignment

En_Core's visual identity follows the Global Visual DNA:

- **Lighting:** Warm cinematic, Rembrandt, deep shadows
- **Palette:** Amber, obsidian, gold, slate grey
- **Style:** Semi-realistic, painterly realism, luxury visual storytelling
- **Mood:** Ancient power, dynasty, hidden machinery

---

## Style Constraints

- Warm cinematic lighting required
- Rembrandt lighting required
- Deep shadow contrast required
- Amber and obsidian primary palette
- Painterly realism over photorealism
- Luxury aesthetic — monumental scale preferred

---

## Negative Prompt

generic anime, pastel palette, flat lighting, oversaturated colors, cartoon, low quality, blurry, watermark, text, logo, bad anatomy, malformed limbs, duplicate body parts, jpeg artifacts, cold lighting, neon glow, cyberpunk palette

input.file_text:
# Hardening Pass Application Checklist
## SvartúlfrVerse | Engine Core

**Use this document when applying the patch to the actual En_Core.js file.**

---

## Pre-Application

- [ ] Save a backup copy of En_Core.js before making any changes
- [ ] Note the current runtime behavior in your test session (basic world routing, POV override, formatting)
- [ ] Confirm En_Core.js is the correct file version before starting

---

## Application Steps

### Step 1 — Header Block

- [ ] Locate the existing header/top of file (any L1 era comment or copyright block)
- [ ] Insert INSERTION POINT 1 block from the patch specification ABOVE all existing code
- [ ] Preserve all existing code below it unchanged

### Step 2 — WORLD_REGISTRY

- [ ] Locate the line: `var mvL0 = {`
- [ ] Insert INSERTION POINT 2 block from the patch specification IMMEDIATELY ABOVE that line
- [ ] Confirm the `var WORLD_REGISTRY = {...}` block is now present
- [ ] Confirm `var mvL0 = {` immediately follows unchanged

### Step 3 — Legacy Routing Comment

- [ ] The INSERTION POINT 2 block includes the legacy routing comment
- [ ] Confirm it appears directly above `var mvL0 = {`
- [ ] Confirm mvL0.dimensions and mvL0.dimensionAliases content is unchanged

### Step 4 — POV Firewall Comment

- [ ] Locate the POV Override / casting enforcement logic
- [ ] Insert INSERTION POINT 3 block from the patch specification IMMEDIATELY ABOVE the firewall
- [ ] Confirm firewall logic is unchanged below the comment

### Step 5 — World Lore DEBT-003 Tags

- [ ] Search for: Douglas Estate
- [ ] Insert DEBT-003 inline comment on the line above
- [ ] Search for: Solarton
- [ ] Insert DEBT-003 inline comment on the line above
- [ ] Search for: Bloodmoon
- [ ] Insert DEBT-003 inline comment on the line above (use W_NorseMythic or W_UrbanFantasy)
- [ ] Search for: Ambrosia
- [ ] Insert DEBT-003 inline comment on the line above

### Step 6 — Context Guards Label

- [ ] Locate the context guard block (context.character = context.character || {})
- [ ] Insert INSERTION POINT 5 block from the patch specification ABOVE the guard block

### Step 7 — End of File

- [ ] Navigate to the very end of En_Core.js
- [ ] Append INSERTION POINT 6 block from the patch specification

---

## Post-Application Verification

### Documentation Check

- [ ] File begins with the new header block
- [ ] WORLD_REGISTRY constant is present above mvL0
- [ ] mvL0.dimensions and mvL0.dimensionAliases are unchanged
- [ ] POV firewall comment is present above firewall logic
- [ ] All four world lore references have DEBT-003 inline comments
- [ ] End-of-file block is present

### Behavioral Verification

- [ ] Load a test session with En_Core active
- [ ] Confirm world routing behaves identically to pre-patch
- [ ] Confirm POV Override functions correctly (assign C_Alyssa, verify no duplicate NPC)
- [ ] Confirm output formatting is unchanged
- [ ] Send 10+ messages and confirm no drift or corruption

### Sign-Off

- [ ] Zero behavioral changes confirmed
- [ ] All documentation insertions confirmed present
- [ ] Backup copy archived

---

## If Something Goes Wrong

1. Restore from backup immediately
2. Do not attempt to fix from the patched version
3. Identify which insertion caused the issue
4. Document the finding in the Technical Debt Report as a new debt item
5. Proceed with the patch minus that specific insertion only

---

*Application Checklist — SvartúlfrVerse Engine Core Hardening Pass*

**Use this document when applying the patch to the actual En_Core.js file.**

---

## Pre-Application

- [ ] Save a backup copy of En_Core.js before making any changes
- [ ] Note the current runtime behavior in your test session (basic world routing, POV override, formatting)
- [ ] Confirm En_Core.js is the correct file version before starting

---

## Application Steps

### Step 1 — Header Block

- [ ] Locate the existing header/top of file (any L1 era comment or copyright block)
- [ ] Insert INSERTION POINT 1 block from the patch specification ABOVE all existing code
- [ ] Preserve all existing code below it unchanged

### Step 2 — WORLD_REGISTRY

- [ ] Locate the line: `var mvL0 = {`
- [ ] Insert INSERTION POINT 2 block from the patch specification IMMEDIATELY ABOVE that line
- [ ] Confirm the `var WORLD_REGISTRY = {...}` block is now present
- [ ] Confirm `var mvL0 = {` immediately follows unchanged

### Step 3 — Legacy Routing Comment

- [ ] The INSERTION POINT 2 block includes the legacy routing comment
- [ ] Confirm it appears directly above `var mvL0 = {`
- [ ] Confirm mvL0.dimensions and mvL0.dimensionAliases content is unchanged

### Step 4 — POV Firewall Comment

- [ ] Locate the POV Override / casting enforcement logic
- [ ] Insert INSERTION POINT 3 block from the patch specification IMMEDIATELY ABOVE the firewall
- [ ] Confirm firewall logic is unchanged below the comment

### Step 5 — World Lore DEBT-003 Tags

- [ ] Search for: Douglas Estate
- [ ] Insert DEBT-003 inline comment on the line above
- [ ] Search for: Solarton
- [ ] Insert DEBT-003 inline comment on the line above
- [ ] Search for: Bloodmoon
- [ ] Insert DEBT-003 inline comment on the line above (use W_NorseMythic or W_UrbanFantasy)
- [ ] Search for: Ambrosia
- [ ] Insert DEBT-003 inline comment on the line above

### Step 6 — Context Guards Label

- [ ] Locate the context guard block (context.character = context.character || {})
- [ ] Insert INSERTION POINT 5 block from the patch specification ABOVE the guard block

### Step 7 — End of File

- [ ] Navigate to the very end of En_Core.js
- [ ] Append INSERTION POINT 6 block from the patch specification

---

## Post-Application Verification

### Documentation Check

- [ ] File begins with the new header block
- [ ] WORLD_REGISTRY constant is present above mvL0
- [ ] mvL0.dimensions and mvL0.dimensionAliases are unchanged
- [ ] POV firewall comment is present above firewall logic
- [ ] All four world lore references have DEBT-003 inline comments
- [ ] End-of-file block is present

### Behavioral Verification

- [ ] Load a test session with En_Core active
- [ ] Confirm world routing behaves identically to pre-patch
- [ ] Confirm POV Override functions correctly (assign C_Alyssa, verify no duplicate NPC)
- [ ] Confirm output formatting is unchanged
- [ ] Send 10+ messages and confirm no drift or corruption

### Sign-Off

- [ ] Zero behavioral changes confirmed
- [ ] All documentation insertions confirmed present
- [ ] Backup copy archived

---

## If Something Goes Wrong

1. Restore from backup immediately
2. Do not attempt to fix from the patched version
3. Identify which insertion caused the issue
4. Document the finding in the Technical Debt Report as a new debt item
5. Proceed with the patch minus that specific insertion only

---

content.text:
Here is the complete hardening pass delivery. Everything above is documentation and annotation only — zero logic changes.

---

## Pass Summary

### What was produced

**OUTPUT 1 — Architecture Audit Report**
Nine findings across four audit sections: header epoch mismatch, absent governance declaration, missing WORLD_REGISTRY, legacy variable naming, L1/L2/L3 comment drift, POV firewall fragility (existing ADR_008 debt), world lore contamination, missing semantic layer map, and an outdated En_Core.md. Runtime risk of the patch: zero across all nine.

**OUTPUT 2 — Technical Debt Report**
Six catalogued debt items with IDs, migration risk ratings, target states, and milestone assignments. Key items: DEBT-002 (POV firewall string parsing, deferred per ADR_008) and DEBT-003 (world lore references in engine layer — highest remediation priority after LA validation). Each item explicitly requires WF_008 before any code changes.

**OUTPUT 3 — En_Core.js Patch Specification**
Six labeled insertion points with exact text to insert. The patch adds: a complete architecture header block, the WORLD_REGISTRY documentation constant (above mvL0, which is left untouched), the legacy routing compatibility comment, the POV firewall documentation block, DEBT-003 inline tags above each lore reference, and the end-of-file summary block. The JS file included in the patch **is the insertion content itself** — it is not meant to replace En_Core.js but to be applied to it using the checklist.

**OUTPUT 4 — Rewritten En_Core.md**
Full replacement per specification: correct title, Midnight theme, updated description, semantic layer contracts, layer dependency diagram, governance hierarchy, full responsibilities including the new WORLD_REGISTRY taxonomy, known technical debt table, related ADR cross-reference, and updated cover prompt with negative prompt refinements. No L1/L2/L3 references appear anywhere.

**OUTPUT 5 — Application Checklist** (bonus)
Step-by-step application guide since En_Core.js was not accessible for direct patching. Covers pre-application, all six insertion steps, behavioral verification, and rollback procedure.

### One flag

En_Core.js was not accessible in the sandbox. The patch specification is written so it can be applied by opening the actual file and following the checklist. Once applied, the behavior verification steps in the checklist confirm zero regression before this pass is considered closed.
